Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000026

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 3-4'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The GPT4AIGChip framework combines an LLM-friendly, decoupled hardware template with a demo-augmented prompt generator to enable automated AI accelerator design via in-context learning. The system runs an iterative four-stage pipeline: (1) a search engine selects the next module and instruction using feedback from prior designs, (2) the demo-augmented prompt generator builds module-level prompts by combining relevant instruction-code demonstrations (instruction-code pairs) to boost in-context learning for HLS, (3) the LLMs sequentially generate module implementations, and (4) a design validation flow inspects and modifies generated code to ensure deployability. The paper emphasizes decoupled templates to avoid long dependencies, hierarchy-based module/sub-module decomposition to limit code complexity per step, and careful prompt engineering—providing high-quality, correlated demonstrations—to activate LLM generalization from HLS examples. Empirical observations note that open-source models (e.g., CodeGen) perform poorly without finetuning (0% Pass@100) and that even two-step finetuning trails closed-source GPT-4 (about 11% lower Pass@100), motivating the use of closed-source powerful LLMs with in-context learning when annotated data are scarce.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 1-1'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The excerpt describes GPT4AIGChip, a framework that demonstrates LLM-powered AI accelerator design automation. Central to the approach is an automated demo-augmented prompt-generation pipeline that leverages in-context learning to guide LLMs toward producing accelerator implementations from natural-language specifications. The authors performed a comprehensive study of LLM capabilities for accelerator design and derived three key insights: (1) existing LLMs have difficulty understanding long, dependency-rich code, especially in infrequently seen languages such as high-level synthesis (HLS), motivating decoupling of hardware functionalities; (2) due to limited annotated data, combining in-context learning with the reasoning strengths of powerful (often closed-source) LLMs is more practical than finetuning open-source models; and (3) augmenting prompts with demos improves guidance for LLMs. The work aims to democratize accelerator design by replacing domain-specific languages with natural language instructions. The excerpt does not include quantitative design-generation results or detailed evaluation metrics, but claims this is the first effective pipeline demonstrating LLM-powered automated AI accelerator generation.


---

## pqac-00000028

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 7-8'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The paper evaluates a demo-augmented prompt generation pipeline (GPT4AIGChip) for automated AI accelerator design generation using LLMs for HLS code synthesis. They perform an ablation over prompt formats and demonstration selection: including explicit demo instructions (demo-augmented) significantly improves generation quality for output-stationary computing units, increasing Pass@10 by 50% over No Demo and 30% over High-Level Description. Pass@10 is used as the evaluation metric. Demonstration selection matters (they compare Random and Dissimilar selections), and the model can identify and fuse reusable code patterns from two demos (e.g., inner-product and outer-product behaviors) to produce logically correct implementations. In automated design results, GPT4AIGChip can outperform a random search baseline and match manual expert designs under comparable DSP/resource constraints, though differences narrow under tight DSP budgets. A key limitation is reliance on a tailored demonstration library, which is costly to construct and reduces cross-domain applicability; the authors suggest collecting annotated hardware design code and descriptions for LLM fine-tuning to encode domain knowledge and reduce dependence on external demos.


---

## pqac-00000029

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 8-9'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The excerpt describes GPT4AIGChip, which uses an automated, demo-augmented prompt-generation pipeline that leverages in-context learning to guide LLMs to produce AI accelerator designs (including a row-wise-product-based implementation visualized in Fig. 8). The authors report experiments and ablation studies that validate GPT4AIGChip’s effectiveness in generating high-performance accelerators from natural-language prompts. They note strengths of in-context learning—providing a few input-output demonstrations improves performance—but also important limitations: token-length constraints (e.g., 4096 tokens for GPT-4) make full designs difficult to include, motivating a decoupled hardware template and careful selection of highly correlated demonstrations. Human intervention remains necessary to rectify module interfaces and assemble the final accelerator due to diverse implementation styles. Verification cost is highlighted as a substantial, unaddressed part of the pipeline; the authors propose future work on verification tools or even a dedicated LLM for verification, and suggest hardware-template design and LLM finetuning to improve consistency and reduce human effort.

## pqac-00000030

From text 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 1-1'.

Kaiyan Chang, Ying Wang, Haimeng Ren, Mengdi Wang, Shengwen Liang, Yinhe Han, Huawei Li, and Xiaowei Li. Chipgpt: how far are we from natural language hardware design. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019. This article has 216 citations.

The excerpt describes ChipGPT, a four-stage, zero-code framework that uses large language models to generate hardware (Verilog) from natural language. The pipeline centers on a Prompt Manager that formulates prompts for the LLM and an Output Manager that corrects and optimizes the LLM-produced Verilog via machine and human feedback, collecting many candidate programs into a design space. An enumerative search stage then explores that design space to select optimal designs with respect to PPA (performance, power, area) metrics. The authors emphasize in-context learning (using prompts and examples) rather than fine-tuning: "we propose a natural language chip logic design method based on in-context learning, which does not modify the large model itself." The paper notes limitations of current LLMs for HDL generation—lack of reliable prompt templates, inability to produce customized modules or apply top-down design—and practical tooling constraints because foundational models often run on cloud servers, hindering fine-tuning. It also situates the work relative to higher-level hardware specification tools such as Chisel and Xilinx HLS. Overall, the method augments LLM outputs with post-processing and search to enable automated RTL/HLS-style design exploration from natural-language specs.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 2-3'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The paper evaluates using HLS implementation templates as prompts to pair user instructions with LLMs for automated AI accelerator generation. They adapt a for-loop-based HLS template and assess GPT-4’s ability to generate hardware implementations, observing that LLMs frequently produce non-synthesizable, functionally incorrect code. Common failures include misinterpretation of variable definitions and instantiating modules with incorrect BRAM/module sizes, which breaks computation tile sizing and data reuse. Hardware-specific coding patterns are often missed; for example, broadcasting a single element requires assigning it to a register-like variable before writing to multiple locations to avoid source-buffer access conflicts, but LLMs often overlook this. The authors discuss a Demo-Augmented Prompt Generator (a demonstration library and prompt pipeline) to improve in-context learning for HLS by pairing instructions with HLS templates/demos. They also note challenges in finetuning: while finetuning open-source LLMs on annotated HLS could help, high-quality HLS+annotation datasets are scarce, and closed-source advanced LLMs are not available for finetuning, motivating the use of demo-augmentation and open models for improved generalization and logical reasoning in hardware design generation.


---

## pqac-00000032

From text 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 8-9'.

Kaiyan Chang, Ying Wang, Haimeng Ren, Mengdi Wang, Shengwen Liang, Yinhe Han, Huawei Li, and Xiaowei Li. Chipgpt: how far are we from natural language hardware design. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019. This article has 216 citations.

The excerpt presents evaluation results and conclusions for ChipGPT, a framework that turns natural language specifications into automatic chip logic design by integrating large language models into EDA tools without retraining. Table VII gives PPA comparisons under an area-optimization target across several workloads; for example, ChipGPT achieves a matrix-multiply entry of "Ours(ChipGPT) 13.14 952.4 1", indicating much lower power and area than the ChatGPT baseline for that case. The paper reports an area reduction of 47% compared with the original ChatGPT in area-target optimization mode and claims code volume reductions of 5.32–9.25× versus traditional agile methods. Generated code quality figures and human-correction assessment show that highlighted incorrect lines are few—"fewer than three" per program—and that corrections are localized. The authors state ChipGPT accelerates chip development, improves LLM correctness (from "probable right to rule right"), and enables natural-language-driven PPA optimization and hardware design flows without model retraining.


---

## pqac-00000033

From text 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 3-4'.

Kaiyan Chang, Ying Wang, Haimeng Ren, Mengdi Wang, Shengwen Liang, Yinhe Han, Huawei Li, and Xiaowei Li. Chipgpt: how far are we from natural language hardware design. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019. This article has 216 citations.

The excerpt describes ChipGPT’s pipeline for converting chip specifications into Verilog using LLMs and highlights prompt-generation and post-processing strategies relevant to demo-augmented pipelines and in-context learning for HLS. ChipGPT treats HDL = ChipGPT(specification) and uses a Specification Split and template-based Prompt Manager (pre-processing) plus an Output Manager and Enumerative Search (post-processing) to control outputs. It leverages in-context learning (ICL) — eliciting responses from a pre-trained LLM without retraining — and acknowledges LLM limitations: they are often unaware of PPA (power, performance, area) and may produce non-ideal programs. To address this, ChipGPT proposes an output manager to push generated designs toward better PPA, a bottom-top method to compose nested modules for scalability, and three control approaches: customize configuration/context prompts, modify the code-query prompt, or directly edit generated programs. Instead of direct program amendment, the system gives GPT multiple trials to produce improved code. These points are directly relevant to designing demo-augmented prompt pipelines, using ICL for HLS, and managing automated AI accelerator generation quality.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000034

From text 'pan2025asurveyof pages 10-12'.

Jingyu Pan, Guanglei Zhou, Chen-Chia Chang, Isaac Jacobson, Jiang Hu, and Yiran Chen. A survey of research in large language models for electronic design automation. Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3715324, doi:10.1145/3715324. This article has 109 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several EDA-specific limitations and mitigation efforts for LLMs. Pre-training corpus composition strongly affects Verilog generation quality: models pre-trained on small Verilog corpora perform substantially better even after supervised fine-tuning, indicating dataset/domain scarcity is a real issue. Agentic frameworks (e.g., RTLFixer, VerilogCoder) that combine Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), ReAct prompting, compile-error databases, and AST-based waveform tracing can significantly improve functional correctness and debugging, raising pass rates markedly versus plain LLM usage. However, RAG systems and LLMs still struggle with EDA-specific terminologies, complex workflows, and domain adaptation for backend tasks. Timing estimation is called out as especially challenging because models must capture relationships between layout, parasitics, and the mathematical precision of static timing analysis—pointing to risks around incorrect timing semantics. ## pqac-00000040

From text 'sheth2506protocolllmrtlbenchmark pages 5-6'.

Arnav Sheth, Ivaxi Sheth, and Mario Fritz. Protocolllm: rtl benchmark for systemverilog generation of communication protocols. ArXiv, Jun 2506. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.07945, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2506.07945. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt documents several concrete failure modes of LLMs when generating RTL/SystemVerilog for communication protocols. Models frequently produce incomplete state transitions and timing violations that are "not detectable through syntax or synthesis checks alone," leading to "subtle protocol-level misbehaviors and logic flaws." Performance is protocol-dependent: simpler protocols (SPI, AXI4-Lite) are generated more reliably, while temporally complex protocols (UART, I2C) expose deeper weaknesses. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) increased functional intent in some cases but "had a higher tendency to fail waveform simulation" and can cause "semantic degradation, where models produce code that is less consistent or structurally flawed." Synthesis-level anomalies — unusually large area or abnormal power profiles — often indicate underlying logic or structural problems in generated RTL. There are also clear domain gaps: many code-finetuned models lack HDL in their training data, producing "out-of-distribution behavior" (e.g., DeepCoder-14B produced Python instead of SystemVerilog). The excerpt does not explicitly mention misuse of blocking vs non-blocking assignments or hallucination of module ports/signals, indicating those failure modes are not directly documented here and represent gaps to check in further evaluation.

The excerpt analyzes recurring LLM failure modes in RTL generation, illustrated by the persistently failing radix2_div and FSM failures (Prob154_fsm_ps2data). Key issues include specification ambiguity (models choosing incompatible but valid variants), cycle-accuracy and FSM mis-sequencing (off-by-one or misordered per-cycle steps and incorrect sequencing of start/active/done), and bit-width arithmetic mistakes (e.g., implementing 8-bit compares when a 9-bit remainder path is required). Finalization steps are often omitted (last quotient/remainder commit), and signed-division sign rules are frequently mishandled. Handshake drift appears as misuse of res_ready/res_valid and confusion between level vs. edge signalling, causing deadlocks or stale outputs. The report also highlights syntax-versus-semantics errors such as using blocking assignments in sequential logic and simplifying borrow-detection to a plain compare. Practical prompt/testbench fixes made during RTLLM included adding missing signals (res_ready), fixing module-name mismatches, clarifying shift vs. rotate semantics, and correcting instantiation order—indicating hallucinated or mismatched ports/signals and prompt misalignment are real issues. The excerpt does not discuss dataset size or Verilog training data scarcity explicitly.

From text 'liu2023chipnemodomainadaptedllms pages 1-2'.

Mingjie Liu, Teodor-Dumitru Ene, Robert Kirby, Chris Cheng, Nathaniel Pinckney, Rongjian Liang, Jonah Alben, Himyanshu Anand, Sanmitra Banerjee, Ismet Bayraktaroglu, Bonita Bhaskaran, Bryan Catanzaro, Arjun Chaudhuri, Sharon Clay, Bill Dally, Laura Dang, Parikshit Deshpande, Siddhanth Dhodhi, Sameer Halepete, Eric Hill, Jiashang Hu, Sumit Jain, Ankit Jindal, Brucek Khailany, George Kokai, Kishor Kunal, Xiaowei Li, Charley Lind, Hao Liu, Stuart Oberman, Sujeet Omar, Ghasem Pasandi, Sreedhar Pratty, Jonathan Raiman, Ambar Sarkar, Zhengjiang Shao, Hanfei Sun, Pratik P Suthar, Varun Tej, Walker Turner, Kaizhe Xu, and Haoxing Ren. Chipnemo: domain-adapted llms for chip design. ArXiv, Oct 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2311.00176, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2311.00176. This article has 342 citations.

The excerpt describes ChipNeMo's domain-adaptive approach: techniques include domain-adaptive tokenization, continued pretraining, instruction alignment, and domain-adapted retrieval. The training/alignment data includes a 24B-token domain-relevant component alongside larger internet/code corpora. The authors trained multiple sizes and report that their largest model, ChipNeMo-70B, "outperforms the highly capable GPT-4 on two of our use cases, namely engineering assistant chatbot and EDA scripts generation," while remaining competitive on bug summarization and analysis. The evaluated applications are an engineering assistant chatbot (for GPU ASIC and architecture engineers), EDA script generation (for two domain-specific tools using Python and Tcl for VLSI timing analysis tasks specified in English), and bug summarization/analysis. The excerpt does not provide numeric accuracy or exact metric values for EDA script generation in these pages; it only states relative performance (ChipNeMo-70B > GPT-4 on the two use cases) and describes the domain-adaptation and alignment process.


---

## pqac-00000049

From text 'sharma2024assessingeconomicviability pages 2-3'.

Assessing Economic Viability: A Comparative Analysis of Total Cost of Ownership for Domain-Adapted Large Language Models versus State-of-the-art Counterparts in Chip Design Coding Assistance This article has 8 citations.

The paper reports ChipNeMo as a domain-adapted LLM (70B parameters) built on LLaMA2. For Domain Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT) the authors used 24 billion tokens overall, although only 120 million tokens were relevant to the EDA tool tasks; processing those 120M tokens took 100 GPU hours. Subsequent supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a curated EDA subset required about 4 GPU hours to process ~5 million tokens. In benchmarked EDA script generation tasks, ChipNeMo outperformed general-purpose models: it achieved 79% accuracy, avoided syntax and logical errors, and had the fastest inference among evaluated models. ChatGPT-4 Turbo (referred to as ChatGPT-4 Turbo) reached about 70% accuracy with a low hallucination rate (~2%), while Anthropic’s Claude 3 had ~68% accuracy and a higher hallucination rate (~11%). The results indicate that domain-adaptive pretraining (even if only a modestly sized task-specific token subset was used) yielded measurable gains in EDA script generation accuracy and robustness compared to general-purpose models.

From text 'liu2023chipnemodomainadaptedllms pages 18-20'.

Mingjie Liu, Teodor-Dumitru Ene, Robert Kirby, Chris Cheng, Nathaniel Pinckney, Rongjian Liang, Jonah Alben, Himyanshu Anand, Sanmitra Banerjee, Ismet Bayraktaroglu, Bonita Bhaskaran, Bryan Catanzaro, Arjun Chaudhuri, Sharon Clay, Bill Dally, Laura Dang, Parikshit Deshpande, Siddhanth Dhodhi, Sameer Halepete, Eric Hill, Jiashang Hu, Sumit Jain, Ankit Jindal, Brucek Khailany, George Kokai, Kishor Kunal, Xiaowei Li, Charley Lind, Hao Liu, Stuart Oberman, Sujeet Omar, Ghasem Pasandi, Sreedhar Pratty, Jonathan Raiman, Ambar Sarkar, Zhengjiang Shao, Hanfei Sun, Pratik P Suthar, Varun Tej, Walker Turner, Kaizhe Xu, and Haoxing Ren. The excerpt reports that domain-adaptive pretraining (DAPT) provides substantial gains: "significant enhancement achieved solely through DAPT training" and DAPT plus model alignment improves domain-specific performance (notably for EDA script generation). SteerLM alignment is helpful on hard benchmarks ("SteerLM training helps on the “hard” benchmark"), while simply adding domain-specific instructional data to SFT gave only marginal benefit and sometimes degraded SteerLM-trained model behavior ("adding domain-specific instructional data to our SFT training led to only marginal improvement on the engineering assistant task." "insufficient adaptation of our data to the SteerLM labeling system"). Retrieval augmentation (RAG) improved accuracy for easy and medium EDA script tasks ("This helped in improving the accuracy of “easy” and “medium” difficulty benchmark") but reduced accuracy on hard tasks ("On the “hard” benchmark, a degradation in the accuracy is noticed as compared to non-RAG model."). Reported evaluation numbers (engineering assistant/chat metrics in the excerpt) show ChipNeMo variants outperforming GPT-4 without retrieval (GPT-4 No = 2.84; ChipNeMo-70B-SteerG No = 5.12; ChipNeMo-70B-Steer No = 4.80). An ablation with GPT-4 using the full 32k context indicates hierarchical summarization had minimal impact. The excerpt does not mention the "24 billion tokens" pretraining quantity, so that specific token-count result is not available here.

RAG consistently boosts scores for all models (numeric gains reported). For EDA script generation, they evaluated Python- and Tcl-based tool benchmarks (sizes listed in Table 2) and found ChipNeMo-70B-Steer significantly outperforms off-the-shelf GPT-4 and LLaMA2-70B-Chat, especially on a proprietary Python tool where general LLMs struggled without API knowledge. The excerpt reports ChipNeMo domain-adaptive pretraining (DAPT) findings but does not provide token-counts like "24 billion tokens" nor explicit EDA script generation accuracy numbers or direct numeric comparisons to GPT-4. Key points: they adapted the LLaMA2 tokenizer adding ~9K tokens, improving tokenization efficiency by ~1.6–3.3% on chip datasets. To mitigate hallucinations in an "engineering assistant chatbot" context they propose retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). DAPT substantially improves in-domain task performance, with larger base models yielding larger in-domain gains; they evaluated 7B, 13B, and 70B models and ran application evaluations using ChipNeMo-70B (aligned via SteerLM). However, applying DAPT directly to a chat-aligned model (LLaMA2-Chat) degraded alignment and made the model unusable for downstream tasks. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA/PEFT) underperformed full-parameter DAPT despite experiments with small and large adapters. Training used 128 A100 GPUs and DAPT accounted for under ~1.5% of overall costs. The excerpt therefore offers process, scale, and qualitative performance insights but lacks the specific quantitative comparisons and EDA generation accuracy asked in the question.

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive summary table of ~15 key publications on LLM-based hardware design and code generation (2022-2025). Columns should include: Reference (short citation), Year, Category (e.g., HDL Generation, Benchmark, Agentic/Self-repair, Analog, Domain Adaptation, Accelerator Design), Key Contribution (1-2 sentences), Benchmark/Evaluation, and Key Metric/Result. Include: (1) ChipGPT - Chang et al. 2023, four-stage zero-code framework, PPA optimization, 47% area reduction; (2) ChipNeMo - Liu et al. 2023, domain-adapted LLM 70B, 24.1B tokens DAPT, outperforms GPT-4 on EDA scripts; (3) VerilogEval - Liu et al. 2023, 156-problem benchmark from HDLBits, pass@k evaluation; (4) RTLLM - Lu et al. 2024, open RTL benchmark with 30 designs, syntax/functionality/quality goals; (5) AutoChip/extended - Blocklove et al. 2025, feedback-driven Verilog generation with EDA tool feedback, 5.8% improvement over zero-shot; (6) MAGE - Zhao et al. 2025, multi-agent RTL engine, 95.7% pass@1 on VerilogEval-V2; (7) RTLFixer - Tsai et al. 2024, RAG+ReAct syntax repair, 98.5% fix rate, 32.3% pass@1 improvement; (8) VeriGen - Thakur et al. 2024, fine-tuned CodeGen-16B on Verilog, open-source; (9) AnalogCoder - Lai et al. 2025, training-free LLM agent for analog circuits via Python/PySpice, 20/24 tasks solved; (10) LaMAGIC - Chang et al. 2024, LM-based analog topology generation, 96% success rate; (11) GPT4AIGChip - Fu et al. 2023, demo-augmented prompt pipeline for AI accelerators, in-context learning for HLS; (12) Chip-Chat - Blocklove et al. 2023, first AI-written HDL tapeout, 8-bit processor; (13) RTLCoder - Liu et al. 2025, open-source 7B model outperforming GPT-3.5; (14) Masala-CHAI - Bhandari et al. 2024, 7500 schematic analog SPICE netlist dataset; (15) Revisiting VerilogEval/VerilogEval v2 - Pinckney et al. 2025, GPT-4o achieves 63% pass rate."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Reference | Year | Category | Key Contribution | Benchmark / Evaluation | Key Metric / Result |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Chang et al., *ChipGPT* | 2023 | HDL Generation / Design-space Search | Introduces a four-stage, zero-code natural-language-to-hardware flow: prompt management, LLM Verilog generation, output correction/optimization, and post-generation search for better PPA. It emphasizes in-context learning rather than retraining and integrates LLM outputs into an EDA-like exploration loop. (pqac-00000030, pqac-00000032) | Natural-language chip logic tasks with PPA comparison against ChatGPT-style baselines; evaluates generated code quality and human correction burden. (pqac-00000032) | Reports up to **47% area reduction** versus original ChatGPT under area-target optimization; human fixes are localized, often fewer than three lines per program. (pqac-00000032) |
| Liu et al., *ChipNeMo* | 2023 | Domain Adaptation / Chip Design Assistant | Domain-adapts LLaMA2 for chip design via tokenizer augmentation, continued pretraining, instruction alignment, and retrieval. Targets engineering assistant chat, EDA script generation, and bug analysis rather than pure RTL generation, but is foundational for hardware-specialized LLMs. (pqac-00000048, pqac-00000054, pqac-00000055) | Evaluated on engineering-assistant QA, Python/Tcl EDA script generation, and bug summarization; uses retrieval-augmented and non-RAG settings. (pqac-00000048, pqac-00000052) | Uses about **24.1B DAPT tokens**; ChipNeMo-70B reportedly **outperforms GPT-4** on engineering assistant and EDA script generation, with script correctness above 70% and +18% improvement from added SFT on one setting. (pqac-00000048, pqac-00000054, pqac-00000055) |
| Liu et al., *VerilogEval* | 2023 | Benchmark | Establishes one of the first standard benchmarks for LLM-based Verilog generation, using HDLBits-derived problems and automatic evaluation via simulation against golden outputs. It made pass@k-style comparison reproducible across models and catalyzed later fine-tuning work. (pqac-00000000) | **156** Verilog problems from HDLBits; functional correctness checked with transient simulation against reference solutions. (pqac-00000000) | Standardizes **pass@k / functional correctness** evaluation for Verilog generation; later work builds directly on it. (pqac-00000000) |
| Lu et al., *RTLLM* | 2024 | Benchmark / RTL Generation | Proposes an open benchmark for natural-language-to-RTL generation with broader attention to design quality, not just correctness. Also introduces self-planning prompting as a simple but effective inference strategy. (pqac-00000005) | Open benchmark with about **30 hand-crafted designs** and three progressive goals: syntax, functionality, and design quality. Syntax is checked by synthesis/netlist validity, functionality by testbenches, and quality by post-synthesis or implementation metrics. (pqac-00000005) | Makes **syntax / functionality / design-quality** triage explicit; self-planning improves GPT-3.5 performance in the benchmark framework. (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000005) |
| Blocklove et al., *AutoChip* / extended TODAES version | 2025 | Agentic / Self-repair | Presents a feedback-driven Verilog generation loop that compiles and simulates LLM outputs, ranks candidates, and iteratively repairs them using EDA feedback. It moves beyond one-shot prompting toward practical self-debugging. (pqac-00000004) | Uses VerilogEval tasks; compares zero-shot generation vs iterative feedback with full-context and succinct-context repair modes. Success is based on compilation and simulation outcomes. (pqac-00000004) | Best case yields **+5.8%** more successful designs than zero-shot and **34.2% lower cost**; mixed-model strategy cuts cost further while preserving gains. (pqac-00000004) |
| Zhao et al., *MAGE* | 2025 | Multi-agent RTL Generation | First open-source multi-agent RTL engine centered on diverse candidate sampling, ranking, debugging, and a state-checkpoint mechanism for targeted fixes. It shows how agent decomposition can dramatically improve RTL correctness. (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000006) | Evaluated on VerilogEval-Human and VerilogEval-V2 with Pass@1 as the main metric, using high-temperature candidate generation and mismatch-based ranking before debugging. (pqac-00000006) | Achieves **94.8% Pass@1** on VerilogEval-Human and **95.7% Pass@1** on VerilogEval-V2 in the high-temperature setting. (pqac-00000006) |
| Tsai et al., *RTLFixer* | 2024 | Agentic / Syntax Repair | Focuses specifically on fixing syntax errors in generated Verilog using Retrieval-Augmented Generation plus ReAct-style iterative reasoning and compiler feedback. It demonstrates that syntax repair alone can unlock large downstream gains in functional pass rates. (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008) | Evaluated on VerilogEval-derived syntax-debug datasets and RTLLM; measures syntax-fix success and downstream VerilogEval pass@1 changes. (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008) | Repairs about **98.5%** of syntax errors; improves pass@1 by **32.3%** on VerilogEval-Machine and **10.1%** on VerilogEval-Human; RTLLM syntax success improves from **73% to 93%**. (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000009) |
| Thakur et al., *VeriGen* | 2024 | HDL Generation / Fine-tuning | Fine-tunes CodeGen-family models on GitHub Verilog and textbook data, showing that small in-house domain models can be competitive with commercial APIs. Also releases open-source models and evaluation assets. (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000046) | Evaluates syntactic and functional correctness using custom test suites and benchmark problems spanning basic to advanced modules. (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000046) | Fine-tuned CodeGen-16B shows **41% improvement in syntactic correctness** over its pretrained counterpart and can slightly exceed GPT-3.5 on the authors’ evaluation. (pqac-00000002) |
| Lai et al., *AnalogCoder* | 2025 | Analog / Agentic Code Generation | Introduces the first training-free LLM agent for analog circuit design by generating Python/PySpice code rather than direct low-level netlists. Combines prompt engineering, feedback-enhanced design flow, and a circuit tool library for reusable subcircuits. (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000016) | Evaluated on a **24-task** analog benchmark with Pass@1 / Pass@5 and solved-task counts; includes basic and composite analog designs. (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000016) | Solves **20/24** tasks, outperforming plain GPT-4o (**15/24**) and other baselines; most successful designs complete within a few attempts. (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000017) |
| Chang et al., *LaMAGIC* | 2024 | Analog Topology Generation | Uses supervised fine-tuning to cast analog topology generation as LM sequence generation over canonical graph/circuit formulations. It is notable for single-pass generation rather than many simulation-search iterations. (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000023) | Evaluates success rate under tolerance thresholds, including a strict **0.01** tolerance; compares multiple circuit encodings including adjacency-matrix-based forms. (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000024) | Reports up to **96% success rate** under strict tolerance 0.01; adjacency-matrix/float-style formulations improve effectiveness on complex circuits. (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021) |
| Fu et al., *GPT4AIGChip* | 2023 | Accelerator Design / HLS | Develops a demo-augmented prompt-generation pipeline for AI accelerator generation, with module decomposition and in-context learning over HLS examples. It explicitly studies why generic LLMs fail on long, dependency-heavy HLS code and proposes hardware-aware prompt structuring. (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000031) | Evaluated through ablations on prompt format and demo selection for HLS accelerator modules; uses Pass@10 and design comparisons under DSP/resource constraints. (pqac-00000028) | Demo-augmented prompting improves **Pass@10 by 50%** over no-demo and **30%** over a high-level-description baseline on one reported module family. (pqac-00000028) |
| Blocklove et al., *Chip-Chat* | 2023 | Conversational Hardware Design / Case Study | Demonstrates conversational co-design of an 8-bit accumulator-based microprocessor with an LLM, culminating in a tapeout. It is a landmark proof-of-concept for AI-assisted hardware co-architecture rather than benchmark-driven RTL generation. (paper_search result: Blocklove et al. 2023) | Real-world case study with SkyWater 130nm/TinyTapeout-style flow; evaluates whether conversational prompting can produce tapeout-ready HDL. | Reported as the **first wholly AI-written HDL sent to tapeout** for an **8-bit processor**. |
| Liu et al., *RTLCoder* | 2025 | Open-source RTL LLM | Presents a compact open-source **7B** RTL-focused model trained on a dedicated RTL dataset and optimized for local/private deployment. It targets practical use as a private engineering assistant. (paper_search result: Liu et al. 2025 RTLCoder) | Evaluated on representative RTL benchmarks including RTLLM and VerilogEval variants. | Reported to **outperform GPT-3.5** on representative RTL benchmarks; quantized 4-bit version can run locally with slight degradation. |
| Bhandari et al., *Masala-CHAI* | 2024 | Analog Dataset / SPICE Netlists | Builds a large-scale schematic-to-SPICE dataset and automated workflow for analog netlist generation from circuit images. Its main value is infrastructure: creating data needed for analog LLM fine-tuning and benchmarking. (paper_search result: Bhandari et al. 2024 Masala-CHAI) | Corpus built from **7,500** schematics across **10 textbooks**; benchmarks open and proprietary LLMs for schematic-to-netlist tasks. | Fine-tuned models used in agentic frameworks such as AnalogCoder show **46% Pass@1 improvement**; dataset is positioned as a key response to analog data scarcity. (paper_search result: Bhandari et al. 2024 Masala-CHAI) |
| Pinckney et al., *Revisiting VerilogEval* | 2025 | Benchmark Update / Evaluation | Updates VerilogEval with better prompt support, failure classification, and spec-to-RTL evaluation. It quantifies the rapid progress of both frontier and domain-specific models over one year. (paper_search result: Pinckney et al. 2025 Revisiting VerilogEval) | Improved VerilogEval v2 benchmark suite for specification-to-RTL and related tasks, with prompt-engineering support and failure analysis. | Reports **GPT-4o at 63% pass rate** on specification-to-RTL tasks; Llama3.1-405B reaches **58%**, and small domain models such as RTL-Coder 6.7B reach **34%**. (paper_search result: Pinckney et al. 2025 Revisiting VerilogEval) |
| Vungarala et al., *SPICEPilot* | 2024 | Analog / SPICE Code Generation | Provides a Python/PySpice dataset and framework for evaluating LLM generation of SPICE simulation code, highlighting that analog code generation remains knowledge-starved and error-prone. It is a useful complementary benchmark/infrastructure paper for analog control electronics generation. (paper_search result: Vungarala et al. 2024 SPICEPilot) | Benchmarks LLM generation of SPICE simulation scripts across different circuit configurations. | Main contribution is benchmark/dataset infrastructure rather than a headline SOTA score; explicitly documents the limitations of current LLMs for SPICE generation. |


*Table: This table summarizes ~15 landmark and representative 2023-2025 publications on LLM-based hardware and circuit generation, including RTL, benchmarks, agentic repair loops, analog design, and accelerator generation. It is useful as a compact map of systems, benchmarks, and headline results for comparing capabilities and limitations across the field.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000038, pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000047, pqac-00000048, pqac-00000049, pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051, pqac-00000052, pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054, pqac-00000055]

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: Find recent (2022-2025) peer-reviewed and arXiv academic publications on large-language-model and code-generation approaches to hardware design: LLM-based generation of HDL (Verilog/VHDL/Chisel), netlists, analog circuit code, and structured hardware description, plus agentic / multi-step design loops and self-repair. Cover landmark and representative systems (e.g., ChipGPT, ChipNeMo, VerilogEval, RTLLM, AutoChip, AnalogCoder, LaMAGIC, MAGE/agentic-EDA, GPT4AIGChip, self-debugging / self-verification loops) and the benchmarks used to evaluate them (functional correctness, synthesizability, pass@k). For each give: full citation, 3-5 sentence summary, and relevance to LLM-driven generation of microcontroller-centric control electronics and firmware for an open-hardware lab-automation device. Discuss capabilities and limitations (correctness, hallucination of parts or pins, verification, dataset scarcity). Aim for ~10-15 references.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

Retrieved Papers:
## 1. ChipGPT: How far are we from natural language hardware design
Valid Text Names:
'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 1-1', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 1-2', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 2-3', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 3-4', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 3-3', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 4-5', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 4-4', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 5-6', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 6-7', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 7-8', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 7-7', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 8-9', 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 9-9'

BibTex:
@article{chang2023chipgpthowfar,
    author = "Chang, Kaiyan and Wang, Ying and Ren, Haimeng and Wang, Mengdi and Liang, Shengwen and Han, Yinhe and Li, Huawei and Li, Xiaowei",
    title = "ChipGPT: How far are we from natural language hardware design",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2305.14019",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
As large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT exhibited unprecedented machine intelligence, it also shows great performance in assisting hardware engineers to realize higher-efficiency logic design via natural language interaction. To estimate the potential of the hardware design process assisted by LLMs, this work attempts to demonstrate an automated design environment that explores LLMs to generate hardware logic designs from natural language specifications. To realize a more accessible and efficient chip development flow, we present a scalable four-stage zero-code logic design framework based on LLMs without retraining or finetuning. At first, the demo, ChipGPT, begins by generating prompts for the LLM, which then produces initial Verilog programs. Second, an output manager corrects and optimizes these programs before collecting them into the final design space. Eventually, ChipGPT will search through this space to select the optimal design under the target metrics. The evaluation sheds some light on whether LLMs can generate correct and complete hardware logic designs described by natural language for some specifications. It is shown that ChipGPT improves programmability, and controllability, and shows broader design optimization space compared to prior work and native LLMs alone.

## 2. QiMeng: Fully Automated Hardware and Software Design for Processor Chip
Valid Text Names:
'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 1-2', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 2-3', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 3-4', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 4-5', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 5-6', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 6-7', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 7-9', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 9-10', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 10-11', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 11-12', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 12-13', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 13-14', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 14-15', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 15-16', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 16-17', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 17-18', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 18-19', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 19-19', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 19-20', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 20-21', 'zhang2025qimengfullyautomated pages 21-21'

    author = "Zhang, Rui and Wen, Yuanbo and Cheng, Shuyao and Huang, Di and Peng, Shaohui and Guo, Jiaming and Jin, Pengwei and Zhao, Jiacheng and Ma, Tianrui and Zhu, Yaoyu and Hao, Yifan and Zhao, Yongwei and Liang, Sheng-rong and Wang, Ying and Hu, Xingui and Du, Zi-Qin and Cui, Huimin and Li, Ling and Guo, Qianwen and Chen, Yunji",
    title = "QiMeng: Fully Automated Hardware and Software Design for Processor Chip",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2506.05007",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2506.05007",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.05007"
}


Abstract:
Processor chip design technology serves as a key frontier driving breakthroughs in computer science and related fields. With the rapid advancement of information technology, conventional design paradigms face three major challenges: the physical constraints of fabrication technologies, the escalating demands for design resources, and the increasing diversity of ecosystems. Automated processor chip design has emerged as a transformative solution to address these challenges. While recent breakthroughs in Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) techniques, have opened new possibilities for fully automated processor chip design, substantial challenges remain in establishing domain-specific LLMs for processor chip design. In this paper, we propose QiMeng, a novel system for fully automated hardware and software design of processor chips. QiMeng comprises three hierarchical layers. In the bottom-layer, we construct a domain-specific Large Processor Chip Model (LPCM) that introduces novel designs in architecture, training, and inference, to address key challenges such as knowledge representation gap, data scarcity, correctness assurance, and enormous solution space. In the middle-layer, leveraging the LPCM's knowledge representation and inference capabilities, we develop the Hardware Design Agent and the Software Design Agent to automate the design of hardware and software for processor chips. Currently, several components of QiMeng have been completed and successfully applied in various top-layer applications, demonstrating significant advantages and providing a feasible solution for efficient, fully automated hardware/software design of processor chips. Future research will focus on integrating all components and performing iterative top-down and bottom-up design processes to establish a comprehensive QiMeng system.

## 4. Hardware Design and Verification with Large Language Models: A Scoping Review, Challenges, and Open Issues

@article{abdollahi2024hardwaredesignand,
    author = "Abdollahi, Meisam and Yeganli, Seyedeh Faegheh and Baharloo, Mohammad (Amir) and Baniasadi, Amirali",
    title = "Hardware Design and Verification with Large Language Models: A Scoping Review, Challenges, and Open Issues",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "Electronics",
    volume = "14",
    pages = "120",
    month = "Dec",
    doi = "10.3390/electronics14010120",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/electronics14010120",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "2079-9292"
}


Abstract:
Background: Large Language Models (LLMs) are emerging as promising tools in hardware design and verification, with recent advancements suggesting they could fundamentally reshape conventional practices. Objective: This study examines the significance of LLMs in shaping the future of hardware design and verification. It offers an extensive literature review, addresses key challenges, and highlights open research questions in this field. Design: in this scoping review, we survey over 360 papers most of the published between 2022 and 2024, including 71 directly relevant ones to the topic, to evaluate the current role of LLMs in advancing automation, optimization, and innovation in hardware design and verification workflows. Results: Our review highlights LLM applications across synthesis, simulation, and formal verification, emphasizing their potential to streamline development processes while upholding high standards of accuracy and performance. We identify critical challenges, such as scalability, model interpretability, and the alignment of LLMs with domain-specific languages and methodologies. Furthermore, we discuss open issues, including the necessity for tailored model fine-tuning, integration with existing Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools, and effective handling of complex data structures typical of hardware projects. Conclusions: this survey not only consolidates existing knowledge but also outlines prospective research directions, underscoring the transformative role LLMs could play in the future of hardware design and verification.

Abstract:
Recent advances in attention-based artificial intelligence (AI) models have unlocked vast potential to automate digital hardware design while enhancing and strengthening security measures against various threats. This rapidly emerging field leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate HDL code, identify vulnerabilities, and sometimes mitigate them. The state of the art in this design automation space utilizes optimized LLMs with HDL datasets, creating automated systems for register-transfer level (RTL) generation, verification, and debugging, and establishing LLM-driven design environments for streamlined logic designs. Additionally, attention-based models like graph attention have shown promise in chip design applications, including floorplanning. This survey investigates the integration of these models into hardware-related domains, emphasizing logic design and hardware security, with or without the use of IP libraries. This study explores the commercial and academic landscape, highlighting technical hurdles and future prospects for automating hardware design and security. Moreover, it provides new insights into the study of LLM-driven design systems, advances in hardware security mechanisms, and the impact of influential works on industry practices. Through the examination of 30 representative approaches and illustrative case studies, this paper underscores the transformative potential of attention-based models in revolutionizing hardware design while addressing the challenges that lie ahead in this interdisciplinary domain.

Abstract:
In the realm of ASIC engineering, the landscape has been significantly reshaped by the rapid development of LLM, paralleled by an increase in the complexity of modern digital circuits. This complexity has escalated the requirements for HDL coding, necessitating a higher degree of precision and sophistication. However, challenges have been faced due to the less-than-optimal performance of modern language models in generating hardware description code, a situation further exacerbated by the scarcity of the corresponding high-quality code datasets. These challenges have highlighted the gap between the potential of LLMs to revolutionize digital circuit design and their current capabilities in accurately interpreting and implementing hardware specifications. To address these challenges, a strategy focusing on the fine-tuning of the leading-edge nature language model and the reshuffling of the HDL code dataset has been developed. The fine-tuning aims to enhance models' proficiency in generating precise and efficient ASIC design, while the dataset reshuffling is intended to broaden the scope and improve the quality of training material. The model demonstrated significant improvements compared to the base model, with approximately 10% to 20% increase in accuracy across a wide range of temperature for the pass@1 metric. This approach is expected to facilitate a simplified and more efficient LLM-assisted framework for complex circuit design, leveraging their capabilities to meet the sophisticated demands of HDL coding and thus streamlining the ASIC development process.

Abstract:
Modern hardware design starts with specifications provided in natural language. These are then translated by hardware engineers into appropriate Hardware Description Languages (HDLs) such as Verilog before synthesizing circuit elements. Automating this translation could reduce sources of human error from the engineering process. But, it is only recently that artificial intelligence (AI) has demonstrated capabilities for machine-based end-to-end design translations. Commercially-available instruction-tuned Large Language Models (LLMs) such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Google’s Bard claim to be able to produce code in a variety of programming languages; but studies examining them for hardware are still lacking. In this work, we thus explore the challenges faced and opportunities presented when leveraging these recent advances in LLMs for hardware design. Given that these ‘conversational’ LLMs perform best when used interactively, we perform a case study where a hardware engineer co-architects a novel 8-bit accumulator-based microprocessor architecture with the LLM according to real-world hardware constraints. We then sent the processor to tapeout in a Skywater 130nm shuttle, meaning that this ‘Chip-Chat’ resulted in what we believe to be the world’s first wholly-AI-written HDL for tapeout.
