    author = "Chaudhuri, Jayeeta and Chaudhuri, Arjun and Chakrabarty, Krishnendu",
    title = "LATENT: LLM-Augmented Trojan Insertion and Evaluation Framework for Analog Netlist Topologies",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD)",
    pages = "133-140",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.06364",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06364"
}


Abstract:
Analog and mixed-signal (A/MS) integrated circuits (ICs) are integral to safety-critical applications. However, the globalization and outsourcing of A/MS ICs to untrusted third-party foundries expose them to security threats, particularly analog Trojans. Unlike digital Trojans which have been extensively studied, analog Trojans remain largely unexplored. There has been only limited research on their diversity and stealth in analog designs, where a Trojan is activated only during a narrow input voltage range. Effective defense techniques require a clear understanding of the attack vectors; however, the lack of diverse analog Trojan instances limits robust advances in detection strategies. To address this gap, we present LATENT, the first large language model (LLM)-driven framework for crafting stealthy, circuit-specific analog Trojans. LATENT incorporates LLM as an autonomous agent to intelligently insert and refine Trojan components within analog designs based on iterative feedback from a detection model. This feedback loop ensures that the inserted Trojans remain stealthy while successfully evading detection. Experimental results demonstrate that our generated Trojan designs exhibit an average Trojan-activation range of 15.74%, ensuring they remain inactive under most operating voltages, while causing a significant performance degradation of 11.3% upon activation.

## 5. Generative AI for Analog Integrated Circuit Design: Methodologies and Applications
Valid Text Names:
'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 1-2', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 2-4', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 4-5', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 5-6', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 6-7', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 7-9', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 9-11', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 11-12', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 13-14', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 14-15', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 15-16', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 16-17', 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 17-17'

    publisher = "Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)",
    issn = "2169-3536"
}


Abstract:
Electronic Design Automation (EDA) in analog Integrated Circuits (ICs) has been the focus of extensive research; however, unlike its digital counterpart, it has not achieved widespread adoption. In this systematic review, we discuss recent contributions in the last five years, highlighting methods that address data scarcity, topology exploration, process-voltage-temperature (PVT) variations, and layout parasitics. Our goal is to support researchers new to this domain by creating a comprehensive collection of references and practical application guidelines. We provide a methodological review of state-of-the-art machine learning (ML) approaches, including graph neural networks (GNNs), large language models (LLMs), and variational autoencoders (VAEs), which have been successfully applied to analog circuit sizing tasks. To the best of authors’ knowledge, this is the first review to comprehensively explore the application of generative AI models in analog IC circuit design. We conclude that future research could focus on few-shot learning with domain-adaptation training of generative AI methods to simplify the design tasks such as human-tool interaction or guided design space exploration.

## 8. AMSnet 2.0: A Large AMS Database with AI Segmentation for Net Detection
Valid Text Names:
'shi2025amsnet2.0a pages 1-2', 'shi2025amsnet2.0a pages 2-3', 'shi2025amsnet2.0a pages 3-4', 'shi2025amsnet2.0a pages 4-6', 'shi2025amsnet2.0a pages 6-7'

    author = "Shi, Yichen and Tao, Zhuofu and Gao, Yuhao and Huang, Li and Wang, Hongyang and Yu, Zhiping and Lin, Ting-Jung and He, Lei",
    title = "AMSnet 2.0: A Large AMS Database with AI Segmentation for Net Detection",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD)",
    pages = "242-248",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.09155",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.09155"
}


Abstract:
Multimodal large language models (MLLM) struggle to understand circuit schematics due to their limited recognition capabilities. This could be attributed to the lack of high-quality schematic-netlist training data. Existing work such as AMSnet applies schematic parsing to generate netlists. However, these methods rely on hard-coded heuristics and are difficult to apply to complex or noisy schematics in this paper. We therefore propose a novel net detection mechanism based on segmentation with high robustness. The proposed method also recovers positional information, allowing digital reconstruction of schematics. We then expand the AMSnet dataset with schematic images from various sources and create AMSnet 2.0. AMSnet 2.0 contains 2,686 circuits with schematic images, Spectre-formatted netlists, OpenAccess digital schematics, and positional information for circuit components and nets, whereas AMSnet only includes 792 circuits with SPICE netlists but no digital schematics.

## 9. DiffCkt: A Diffusion Model-Based Hybrid Neural Network Framework for Automatic Transistor-Level Generation of Analog Circuits
Valid Text Names:
'liu2025diffcktadiffusion pages 1-2', 'liu2025diffcktadiffusion pages 2-3', 'liu2025diffcktadiffusion pages 3-4', 'liu2025diffcktadiffusion pages 4-6', 'liu2025diffcktadiffusion pages 6-7', 'liu2025diffcktadiffusion pages 7-8', 'liu2025diffcktadiffusion pages 8-9', 'liu2025diffcktadiffusion pages 9-9'

    author = "Liu, Chengjie and Li, Jiajia and Feng, Yabing and Huang, Wenhao and Chen, Weiyu and Du, Yuan and Yang, Jun and Du, Li",
    title = "DiffCkt: A Diffusion Model-Based Hybrid Neural Network Framework for Automatic Transistor-Level Generation of Analog Circuits",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2507.00444",
    month = "Jul",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2507.00444",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2507.00444"
}


Abstract:
Analog circuit design consists of the pre-layout and layout phases. Among them, the pre-layout phase directly decides the final circuit performance, but heavily depends on experienced engineers to do manual design according to specific application scenarios. To overcome these challenges and automate the analog circuit pre-layout design phase, we introduce DiffCkt: a diffusion model-based hybrid neural network framework for the automatic transistor-level generation of analog circuits, which can directly generate corresponding circuit structures and device parameters tailored to specific performance requirements. To more accurately quantify the efficiency of circuits generated by DiffCkt, we introduce the Circuit Generation Efficiency Index (CGEI), which is determined by both the figure of merit (FOM) of a single generated circuit and the time consumed. Compared with relative research, DiffCkt has improved CGEI by a factor of $2.21 \sim 8365\times$, reaching a state-of-the-art (SOTA) level. In conclusion, this work shows that the diffusion model has the remarkable ability to learn and generate analog circuit structures and device parameters, providing a revolutionary method for automating the pre-layout design of analog circuits. The circuit dataset will be open source, its preview version is available at https://github.com/CjLiu-NJU/DiffCkt.

    author = "Fu, Yonggan and Zhang, Yongan and Yu, Zhongzhi and Li, Sixu and Ye, Zhifan and Li, Chaojian and Wan, Cheng and Lin, Yingyan",
    title = "GPT4AIGChip: Towards Next-Generation AI Accelerator Design Automation via Large Language Models",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD)",
    pages = "1-9",

    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
The remarkable capabilities and intricate nature of Artificial Intelligence (AI) have dramatically escalated the imperative for specialized AI accelerators. Nonetheless, designing these accelerators for various AI workloads remains both labor- and time-intensive. While existing design exploration and automation tools can partially alleviate the need for extensive human involvement, they still demand substantial hardware expertise, posing a barrier to non-experts and stifling AI accelerator development. Motivated by the astonishing potential of large language models (LLMs) for generating high-quality content in response to human language instructions, we embark on this work to examine the possibility of harnessing LLMs to automate AI accelerator design. Through this endeavor, we develop GPT4AIGChip, a framework intended to democratize AI accelerator design by leveraging human natural languages instead of domain-specific languages. Specifically, we first perform an in-depth investigation into LLMs' limitations and capabilities for AI accelerator design, thus aiding our understanding of our current position and garnering insights into LLM-powered automated AI accelerator design. Furthermore, drawing inspiration from the above insights, we develop a framework called GPT4AIGChip, which features an automated demo-augmented prompt-generation pipeline utilizing in-context learning to guide LLMs towards creating high-quality AI accelerator design. To our knowledge, this work is the first to demonstrate an effective pipeline for LLM-powered automated AI accelerator generation. Accordingly, we anticipate that our insights and framework can serve as a catalyst for innovations in next-generation LLM-powered design automation tools.

    title = "AnalogTester: A Large Language Model-Based Framework for Automatic Testbench Generation in Analog Circuit Design",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "2025 International Symposium of Electronics Design Automation (ISEDA)",
    pages = "201-207",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2507.09965",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2507.09965"
}


Abstract:
Recent advancements have demonstrated the significant potential of large language models (LLMs) in analog circuit design. Nevertheless, testbench construction for analog circuits remains manual, creating a critical bottleneck in achieving fully automated design processes. Particularly when replicating circuit designs from academic papers, manual Testbench construction demands time-intensive implementation and frequent adjustments, which fails to address the dynamic diversity and flexibility requirements for automation. AnalogTester tackles automated analog design challenges through an LLM-powered pipeline: a) domain-knowledge integration, b) paper information extraction, c) simulation scheme synthesis, and d) testbench code generation with Tsinghua Electronic Design (TED). AnalogTester has demonstrated automated Testbench generation capabilities for three fundamental analog circuit types: operational amplifiers (op-amps), bandgap references (BGRs), and low-dropout regulators (LDOs), while maintaining a scalable framework for adaptation to broader circuit topologies. Furthermore, AnalogTester can generate circuit knowledge data and TED code corpus, establishing fundamental training datasets for LLM specialization in analog circuit design automation.

## 4. AnalogCoder: Analog Circuit Design via Training-Free Code Generation

BibTex:
@article{lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit,
    author = "Lai, Yao and Lee, Sungyoung and Chen, Guojin and Poddar, Souradip and Hu, Mengkang and Pan, David Z. and Luo, Ping",
    title = "AnalogCoder: Analog Circuit Design via Training-Free Code Generation",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2405.14918",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2405.14918",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2405.14918",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Analog circuit design is a significant task in modern chip technology, focusing on the selection of component types, connectivity, and parameters to ensure proper circuit functionality. Despite advances made by Large Language Models (LLMs) in digital circuit design, the complexity and scarcity of data in analog circuitry pose significant challenges. To mitigate these issues, we introduce AnalogCoder, the first training-free LLM agent for designing analog circuits through Python code generation. Firstly, AnalogCoder incorporates a feedback-enhanced flow with tailored domain-specific prompts, enabling the automated and self-correcting design of analog circuits with a high success rate. Secondly, it proposes a circuit tool library to archive successful designs as reusable modular sub-circuits, simplifying composite circuit creation. Thirdly, extensive experiments on a benchmark designed to cover a wide range of analog circuit tasks show that AnalogCoder outperforms other LLM-based methods. It has successfully designed 20 circuits, 5 more than standard GPT-4o. We believe AnalogCoder can significantly improve the labor-intensive chip design process, enabling non-experts to design analog circuits efficiently.

## 5. A Survey of Circuit Foundation Model: Foundation AI Models for VLSI Circuit Design and EDA

BibTex:
@article{fang2025asurveyof,
    author = "Fang, Wenji and Wang, Jing and Lu, Yao and Liu, Shang and Wu, Yuchao and Ma, Yuzhe and Xie, Zhiyao",
    title = "A Survey of Circuit Foundation Model: Foundation AI Models for VLSI Circuit Design and EDA",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2504.03711",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711"
}


Abstract:
Artificial intelligence (AI)-driven electronic design automation (EDA) techniques have been extensively explored for VLSI circuit design applications. Most recently, foundation AI models for circuits have emerged as a new technology trend. Unlike traditional task-specific AI solutions, these new AI models are developed through two stages: 1) self-supervised pre-training on a large amount of unlabeled data to learn intrinsic circuit properties; and 2) efficient fine-tuning for specific downstream applications, such as early-stage design quality evaluation, circuit-related context generation, and functional verification. This new paradigm brings many advantages: model generalization, less reliance on labeled circuit data, efficient adaptation to new tasks, and unprecedented generative capability. In this paper, we propose referring to AI models developed with this new paradigm as circuit foundation models (CFMs). This paper provides a comprehensive survey of the latest progress in circuit foundation models, unprecedentedly covering over 130 relevant works. Over 90% of our introduced works were published in or after 2022, indicating that this emerging research trend has attracted wide attention in a short period. In this survey, we propose to categorize all existing circuit foundation models into two primary types: 1) encoder-based methods performing general circuit representation learning for predictive tasks; and 2) decoder-based methods leveraging large language models (LLMs) for generative tasks. For our introduced works, we cover their input modalities, model architecture, pre-training strategies, domain adaptation techniques, and downstream design applications. In addition, this paper discussed the unique properties of circuits from the data perspective. These circuit properties have motivated many works in this domain and differentiated them from general AI techniques.

Abstract:
Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) has emerged as a pivotal focus in global innovation agendas, revealing transformative potential that extends beyond technological applications to reshape diverse societal domains. Given the fundamental dependency of GenAI deployment on circuits and systems (CAS), a co-evolutionary approach integrating both technological paradigms becomes imperative. This synergistic framework confronts three interrelated challenges: 1) developing deployment-ready GenAI algorithms, 2) engineering implementation-efficient CAS architectures, and 3) leveraging GenAI for autonomous CAS designs - each representing critical innovations vectors. Given the rapid advancement of GenAI-CAS technologies, a comprehensive synthesis has become an urgent priority across academia and industry. Consequently, this timely review systematically analyzes current advancements, provides integrative perspectives, and identifies emerging research trajectories. This review endeavors to serve both AI and CAS communities, thereby catalyzing an innovation feedback loop: GenAI-optimized CAS architectures in turn accelerate GenAI evolution through algorithm-hardware co-empowerment.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Large Language Models (LLMs) are emerging as promising tools in hardware design and verification, with recent advancements suggesting they could fundamentally reshape conventional practices. In this survey, we analyze over 54 research papers to assess the current role of LLMs in enhancing automation, optimization, and innovation within hardware design and verification workflows. Our review highlights LLM applications across synthesis, simulation, and formal verification, emphasizing their potential to streamline development processes while upholding high standards of accuracy and performance. We identify critical challenges, such as scalability, model interpretability, and the alignment of LLMs with domain-specific languages and methodologies. Furthermore, we discuss open issues, including the necessity for tailored model fine-tuning, integration with existing Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools, and effective handling of complex data structures typical of hardware projects. This survey not only consolidates existing knowledge but also outlines prospective research directions, underscoring the transformative role LLMs could play in the future of hardware design and verification.</jats:p>

## 8. LLM-IMC: Automating Analog In-Memory Computing Architecture Generation with Large Language Models
Valid Text Names:
'vungarala2025llmimcautomatinganalog pages 1-1'

@article{vungarala2025llmimcautomatinganalog,
    author = "Vungarala, Deepak and Amin, Md Hasibul and Mercati, Pietro and Roohi, Arman and Zand, Ramtin and Angizi, Shaahin",
    title = "LLM-IMC: Automating Analog In-Memory Computing Architecture Generation with Large Language Models",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "2025 IEEE 33rd Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)",
    pages = "292-292",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.1109/fccm62733.2025.00071",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1109/fccm62733.2025.00071",
    publisher = "IEEE"
}


Abstract:
Resistive crossbars enabling analog In-Memory Computing (IMC) have garnered significant attention from academia and industry as a promising architecture for Deep Neural Network (DNN) acceleration, thanks to their high memory access bandwidth and in-situ computing capabilities. However, the knowledge-intensive hardware design process and the lack of high-quality circuit netlists have constrained design space exploration and optimization of analog IMC to behavioral system-level tools. In this one-page abstract, we introduce LLM-IMC, a novel fine-tune-free Large Language Model (LLM) framework, supported by a Python-based tool, designed for analog IMC SPICE code generation. LLM-IMC systematically addresses these limitations by automating the creation of diverse IMC simulation scripts, enabling efficient design space exploration through LLM-driven performance, and outlining an integration roadmap for hardware-oriented neuromorphic crossbar design flows.

    author = "Chang, Chen-Chia and Shen, Yikang and Fan, Shaoze and Li, Jing and Zhang, Shun and Cao, Ningyuan and Chen, Yiran and Zhang, Xin",
    title = "LaMAGIC: Language-Model-based Topology Generation for Analog Integrated Circuits",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2407.18269",
    month = "Jul",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
In the realm of electronic and electrical engineering, automation of analog circuit is increasingly vital given the complexity and customized requirements of modern applications. However, existing methods only develop search-based algorithms that require many simulation iterations to design a custom circuit topology, which is usually a time-consuming process. To this end, we introduce LaMAGIC, a pioneering language model-based topology generation model that leverages supervised finetuning for automated analog circuit design. LaMAGIC can efficiently generate an optimized circuit design from the custom specification in a single pass. Our approach involves a meticulous development and analysis of various input and output formulations for circuit. These formulations can ensure canonical representations of circuits and align with the autoregressive nature of LMs to effectively addressing the challenges of representing analog circuits as graphs. The experimental results show that LaMAGIC achieves a success rate of up to 96\% under a strict tolerance of 0.01. We also examine the scalability and adaptability of LaMAGIC, specifically testing its performance on more complex circuits. Our findings reveal the enhanced effectiveness of our adjacency matrix-based circuit formulation with floating-point input, suggesting its suitability for handling intricate circuit designs. This research not only demonstrates the potential of language models in graph generation, but also builds a foundational framework for future explorations in automated analog circuit design.

## 2. LaMAGIC2: Advanced Circuit Formulations for Language Model-Based Analog Topology Generation

    title = "LaMAGIC2: Advanced Circuit Formulations for Language Model-Based Analog Topology Generation",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2506.10235",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2506.10235",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.10235"
}


Abstract:
Automation of analog topology design is crucial due to customized requirements of modern applications with heavily manual engineering efforts. The state-of-the-art work applies a sequence-to-sequence approach and supervised finetuning on language models to generate topologies given user specifications. However, its circuit formulation is inefficient due to O(|V |2) token length and suffers from low precision sensitivity to numeric inputs. In this work, we introduce LaMAGIC2, a succinct float-input canonical formulation with identifier (SFCI) for language model-based analog topology generation. SFCI addresses these challenges by improving component-type recognition through identifier-based representations, reducing token length complexity to O(|V |), and enhancing numeric precision sensitivity for better performance under tight tolerances. Our experiments demonstrate that LaMAGIC2 achieves 34% higher success rates under a tight tolerance of 0.01 and 10X lower MSEs compared to a prior method. LaMAGIC2 also exhibits better transferability for circuits with more vertices with up to 58.5% improvement. These advancements establish LaMAGIC2 as a robust framework for analog topology generation.

## 3. AnalogFed: Federated Discovery of Analog Circuit Topologies with Generative AI
Valid Text Names:
'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 1-2', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 2-4', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 2-2', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 4-4', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 4-5', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 5-6', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 6-7', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 7-8', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 8-9', 'li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery pages 9-9'

BibTex:
@article{li2025analogfedfederateddiscovery,
    author = "Li, Qiufeng and Hong, Shu and Gao, Jian and Zhang, Xuan and Lan, Tian and Cao, Weidong",
    title = "AnalogFed: Federated Discovery of Analog Circuit Topologies with Generative AI",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2507.15104",
    month = "Jul",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2507.15104",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2507.15104"
}


Abstract:
Recent breakthroughs in AI/ML offer exciting opportunities to revolutionize analog design automation through data-driven approaches. In particular, researchers are increasingly fascinated by harnessing the power of generative AI to automate the discovery of novel analog circuit topologies. Unlocking the full potential of generative AI in these data-driven discoveries requires access to large and diverse datasets.Yet, there is a significant barrier in the analog domain--Analog circuit design is inherently proprietary, involving not only confidential circuit structures but also the underlying commercial semiconductor processes. As a result, current generative AI research is largely confined to individual researchers who construct small, narrowly focused private datasets. This fragmentation severely limits collaborative innovation and impedes progress across the research community. To address these challenges, we propose AnalogFed. AnalogFed enables collaborative topology discovery across decentralized clients (e.g., individual researchers or institutions) without requiring the sharing of raw private data. To make this vision practical, we introduce a suite of techniques tailored to the unique challenges of applying FedL in analog design--from generative model development and data heterogeneity handling to privacy-preserving strategies that ensure both flexibility and security for circuit designers and semiconductor manufacturers. Extensive experiments across varying client counts and dataset sizes demonstrate that AnalogFed achieves performance comparable to centralized baselines--while maintaining strict data privacy. Specifically, the generative AI model within AnalogFed achieves state-of-the-art efficiency and scalability in the design of analog circuit topologies.

function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'pan2025asurveyof pages 6-8'.

Jingyu Pan, Guanglei Zhou, Chen-Chia Chang, Isaac Jacobson, Jiang Hu, and Yiran Chen. A survey of research in large language models for electronic design automation. Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3715324, doi:10.1145/3715324. This article has 109 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt summarizes many LLM-for-HDL efforts and their evaluation approaches but does not provide numeric pass@k scores. ChipGPT is described as a four-stage, zero-code framework using in-context learning with GPT-3.5 to generate Verilog from natural-language specs and integrate into EDA flows without retraining. Benchmarking/evaluation frameworks mentioned include PyHDL-Eval (first evaluation framework for Python-embedded DSLs) and VerilogEval (comprehensive Verilog benchmark/dataset used to show benefits from supervised fine-tuning). RTLLM provides an open-source RTL-generation benchmark and introduces a self-planning prompt technique that improves GPT-3.5 performance. RTLCoder is an efficient open-source LLM claiming superior performance to GPT-3.5 via domain-adaptive training. Decoding customization (MCTS) is reported to improve functional correctness and PPA. VerilogReader couples LLMs with coverage-directed test generation via Coverage and DUT explainers. AutoChip iteratively refines generated Verilog using compilation/simulation feedback. MEIC uses dual-agent (GPT-4 Turbo) debugging and scoring. The excerpt records methods (in-context learning, DAPT/SFT, decoding changes, retrieval/RAG, ReAct, iterative EDA feedback) and common correctness evaluations (compilation/simulation feedback, functional tests, coverage metrics, comparative benchmarks). It lacks detailed numeric results, pass@k, and explicit benchmark numbers for ChipNeMo, MAGE, RTLFixer, AnalogCoder, LaMAGIC, and GPT4AIGChip.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'ravindran2025surveyandbenchmarking pages 5-7'.

Arun Ravindran, Aditya Patra, Vahid Babaey, and Suresh Purini. Survey and benchmarking of large language models for rtl code generation: techniques and open challenges. Unknown journal, Sep 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202509.1681.v1, doi:10.20944/preprints202509.1681.v1.

The excerpt summarizes multiple RTL-focused LLM projects and benchmarks. ChipNeMo is a domain-adapted 70B model from NVIDIA using continued pre-training and instruction alignment for chip-design tasks (EDA script generation, bug summarisation), though no pass@k or numeric accuracy is reported. AutoChip is described as an early ReAct framework that feeds compiler and simulation errors back to the LLM for self-correction (no scores reported). The excerpt describes AutoChip: an open-source framework that iteratively generates and improves Verilog using LLMs plus EDA tool feedback. Evaluation uses the VerilogEval benchmarks (from HDLBits) and compares two feedback modes—"full context" (append entire conversation) and "succinct" (only most recent iteration feedback) to manage model context windows and token costs. AutoChip issues a natural-language design prompt plus a Verilog module signature and testbench; the LLM generates multiple candidate implementations which are compiled/simulated and ranked. Ranking: response missing a module = -2, fails to compile = -1, compilation warnings = -0.5, simulation rank = proportion of correct test outputs; a candidate that passes all tests is successful and ends the run. AutoChip uses greedy tree search to follow the best branch and measures both token cost and USD cost of model usage. The paper evaluates multiple commercial LLMs (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-Mini, GPT-3.5-Turbo, Claude 3 Haiku) and compares feedback-driven generation to zero-shot baselines. The authors provide an open dataset and analyze how iterations, context size, and mixed-model strategies affect correctness and cost.

The excerpt describes RTLFixer, a framework that combines Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with LLMs used as autonomous agents (ReAct) to target syntax error correction in Verilog RTL code. RTLFixer uses a retrieval database of stored compiler messages and human expert guidance to provide explicit guidance during debugging. The authors created VerilogEval-syntax (174 erroneous implementations) for Verilog syntax debugging. Reported results include an overall 98.5% success rate for resolving syntax errors, and addressing syntax errors alone yields a 32.3% improvement in pass@1 on VerilogEval-Machine and a 10.1% improvement on VerilogEval-Human. The approach also generalizes: syntax success on the RTLLM benchmark improved from 73% to 93%. The paper highlights that combining ReAct (reasoning + actions) with RAG and human guidance produces these substantial gains in syntax-fix performance and downstream pass@1 metrics.

AnalogCoder is presented as the first training-free LLM agent that designs analog circuits by generating Python code from natural-language specifications. To address analog-specific difficulties (complexity, low data availability, and low abstraction levels), the system uses a feedback-enhanced flow with tailored domain-specific prompts to enable automated, self-correcting design. It also includes a circuit tool library that archives successful designs as reusable modular sub-circuits, simplifying construction of composite circuits. Extensive benchmark experiments show AnalogCoder outperforms other LLM-based methods: it successfully designed 20 circuits (five more than standard GPT-4o) on a wide-ranging benchmark. The authors highlight the goal of reducing expert labor and enabling non-experts to produce analog designs; code and the benchmark are provided on GitHub. The paper frames analog circuit design as significantly more challenging than digital design, motivating the training-free, Python-code-centered approach and the emphasis on feedback and modular reuse.

Chen-Chia Chang, Yikang Shen, Shaoze Fan, Jing Li, Shun Zhang, Ningyuan Cao, Yiran Chen, and Xin Zhang. Lamagic: language-model-based topology generation for analog integrated circuits. ArXiv, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269. This article has 70 citations.

The excerpt reports that LaMAGIC is an LM-based, single-pass topology generation system for analog circuit design that uses supervised fine-tuning (SFT). The authors developed canonical input/output formulations compatible with autoregressive LMs to represent circuits as graphs. Their experimental results show the proposed formulations outperform a naive formulation, achieving a success rate up to 0.96 under a strict tolerance of 0.01. They evaluated scalability and adaptability on more complex six-component circuits; the adjacency-matrix-based circuit formulation with float input to the LM is highlighted as offering better effectiveness for complex circuit understanding. The paper also notes different formulation variants: FM and PM exhibit greater robustness and less overfitting than CF and CFDC, with FM/PM achieving higher success rates in transferability experiments on complex circuits. The work emphasizes that appropriate formulation (adjacency matrix + float inputs) and SFT enable high-accuracy topology generation and suggests extending LaMAGIC to a wider range of analog components in future work.

The method can efficiently generate an optimized circuit design in a single pass ("One iteration! Success rate = 96%") and reports experimental results showing up to a 96% success rate under a strict tolerance of 0.01. The authors additionally highlight an adjacency matrix–based circuit formulation, which they find enhances effectiveness when handling intricate, more complex circuit designs. The excerpt contrasts prior LaMAGIC-style language-model methods (sequence-to-sequence finetuning using an adjacency-matrix circuit formulation) with the new LaMAGIC2 approach. Table 1 shows much shorter average token lengths for LaMAGIC2-SFCI (36.40 / 41.41 for 3–5 and 6-comp circuits) versus LaMAGIC-FM (84.79 / 105.00), indicating substantial input size reduction. ## pqac-00000026

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 3-4'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The GPT4AIGChip framework combines an LLM-friendly, decoupled hardware template with a demo-augmented prompt generator to enable automated AI accelerator design via in-context learning. The system runs an iterative four-stage pipeline: (1) a search engine selects the next module and instruction using feedback from prior designs, (2) the demo-augmented prompt generator builds module-level prompts by combining relevant instruction-code demonstrations (instruction-code pairs) to boost in-context learning for HLS, (3) the LLMs sequentially generate module implementations, and (4) a design validation flow inspects and modifies generated code to ensure deployability. The paper emphasizes decoupled templates to avoid long dependencies, hierarchy-based module/sub-module decomposition to limit code complexity per step, and careful prompt engineering—providing high-quality, correlated demonstrations—to activate LLM generalization from HLS examples. Empirical observations note that open-source models (e.g., CodeGen) perform poorly without finetuning (0% Pass@100) and that even two-step finetuning trails closed-source GPT-4 (about 11% lower Pass@100), motivating the use of closed-source powerful LLMs with in-context learning when annotated data are scarce.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 1-1'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The excerpt describes GPT4AIGChip, a framework that demonstrates LLM-powered AI accelerator design automation. Central to the approach is an automated demo-augmented prompt-generation pipeline that leverages in-context learning to guide LLMs toward producing accelerator implementations from natural-language specifications. The authors performed a comprehensive study of LLM capabilities for accelerator design and derived three key insights: (1) existing LLMs have difficulty understanding long, dependency-rich code, especially in infrequently seen languages such as high-level synthesis (HLS), motivating decoupling of hardware functionalities; (2) due to limited annotated data, combining in-context learning with the reasoning strengths of powerful (often closed-source) LLMs is more practical than finetuning open-source models; and (3) augmenting prompts with demos improves guidance for LLMs. The work aims to democratize accelerator design by replacing domain-specific languages with natural language instructions. The excerpt does not include quantitative design-generation results or detailed evaluation metrics, but claims this is the first effective pipeline demonstrating LLM-powered automated AI accelerator generation.

## pqac-00000028

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 7-8'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The paper evaluates a demo-augmented prompt generation pipeline (GPT4AIGChip) for automated AI accelerator design generation using LLMs for HLS code synthesis. They perform an ablation over prompt formats and demonstration selection: including explicit demo instructions (demo-augmented) significantly improves generation quality for output-stationary computing units, increasing Pass@10 by 50% over No Demo and 30% over High-Level Description. Pass@10 is used as the evaluation metric. Demonstration selection matters (they compare Random and Dissimilar selections), and the model can identify and fuse reusable code patterns from two demos (e.g., inner-product and outer-product behaviors) to produce logically correct implementations. In automated design results, GPT4AIGChip can outperform a random search baseline and match manual expert designs under comparable DSP/resource constraints, though differences narrow under tight DSP budgets. A key limitation is reliance on a tailored demonstration library, which is costly to construct and reduces cross-domain applicability; the authors suggest collecting annotated hardware design code and descriptions for LLM fine-tuning to encode domain knowledge and reduce dependence on external demos.

URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The excerpt describes GPT4AIGChip, which uses an automated, demo-augmented prompt-generation pipeline that leverages in-context learning to guide LLMs to produce AI accelerator designs (including a row-wise-product-based implementation visualized in Fig. 8). The authors report experiments and ablation studies that validate GPT4AIGChip’s effectiveness in generating high-performance accelerators from natural-language prompts. They note strengths of in-context learning—providing a few input-output demonstrations improves performance—but also important limitations: token-length constraints (e.g., 4096 tokens for GPT-4) make full designs difficult to include, motivating a decoupled hardware template and careful selection of highly correlated demonstrations. Human intervention remains necessary to rectify module interfaces and assemble the final accelerator due to diverse implementation styles. Verification cost is highlighted as a substantial, unaddressed part of the pipeline; the authors propose future work on verification tools or even a dedicated LLM for verification, and suggest hardware-template design and LLM finetuning to improve consistency and reduce human effort.

## pqac-00000030

From text 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 1-1'.

Kaiyan Chang, Ying Wang, Haimeng Ren, Mengdi Wang, Shengwen Liang, Yinhe Han, Huawei Li, and Xiaowei Li. Chipgpt: how far are we from natural language hardware design. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019. This article has 216 citations.

The excerpt describes ChipGPT, a four-stage, zero-code framework that uses large language models to generate hardware (Verilog) from natural language. The pipeline centers on a Prompt Manager that formulates prompts for the LLM and an Output Manager that corrects and optimizes the LLM-produced Verilog via machine and human feedback, collecting many candidate programs into a design space. An enumerative search stage then explores that design space to select optimal designs with respect to PPA (performance, power, area) metrics. The authors emphasize in-context learning (using prompts and examples) rather than fine-tuning: "we propose a natural language chip logic design method based on in-context learning, which does not modify the large model itself." The paper notes limitations of current LLMs for HDL generation—lack of reliable prompt templates, inability to produce customized modules or apply top-down design—and practical tooling constraints because foundational models often run on cloud servers, hindering fine-tuning. It also situates the work relative to higher-level hardware specification tools such as Chisel and Xilinx HLS. Overall, the method augments LLM outputs with post-processing and search to enable automated RTL/HLS-style design exploration from natural-language specs.

## pqac-00000031

From text 'fu2023gpt4aigchiptowardsnextgeneration pages 2-3'.

Yonggan Fu, Yongan Zhang, Zhongzhi Yu, Sixu Li, Zhifan Ye, Chaojian Li, Cheng Wan, and Yingyan Lin. Gpt4aigchip: towards next-generation ai accelerator design automation via large language models. 2023 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD), pages 1-9, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.10730. This article has 185 citations.

The paper evaluates using HLS implementation templates as prompts to pair user instructions with LLMs for automated AI accelerator generation. They adapt a for-loop-based HLS template and assess GPT-4’s ability to generate hardware implementations, observing that LLMs frequently produce non-synthesizable, functionally incorrect code. Common failures include misinterpretation of variable definitions and instantiating modules with incorrect BRAM/module sizes, which breaks computation tile sizing and data reuse. Hardware-specific coding patterns are often missed; for example, broadcasting a single element requires assigning it to a register-like variable before writing to multiple locations to avoid source-buffer access conflicts, but LLMs often overlook this. The authors discuss a Demo-Augmented Prompt Generator (a demonstration library and prompt pipeline) to improve in-context learning for HLS by pairing instructions with HLS templates/demos. They also note challenges in finetuning: while finetuning open-source LLMs on annotated HLS could help, high-quality HLS+annotation datasets are scarce, and closed-source advanced LLMs are not available for finetuning, motivating the use of demo-augmentation and open models for improved generalization and logical reasoning in hardware design generation.

From text 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 8-9'.

Kaiyan Chang, Ying Wang, Haimeng Ren, Mengdi Wang, Shengwen Liang, Yinhe Han, Huawei Li, and Xiaowei Li. Chipgpt: how far are we from natural language hardware design. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019. This article has 216 citations.

The excerpt presents evaluation results and conclusions for ChipGPT, a framework that turns natural language specifications into automatic chip logic design by integrating large language models into EDA tools without retraining. Table VII gives PPA comparisons under an area-optimization target across several workloads; for example, ChipGPT achieves a matrix-multiply entry of "Ours(ChipGPT) 13.14 952.4 1", indicating much lower power and area than the ChatGPT baseline for that case. The paper reports an area reduction of 47% compared with the original ChatGPT in area-target optimization mode and claims code volume reductions of 5.32–9.25× versus traditional agile methods. Generated code quality figures and human-correction assessment show that highlighted incorrect lines are few—"fewer than three" per program—and that corrections are localized. The authors state ChipGPT accelerates chip development, improves LLM correctness (from "probable right to rule right"), and enables natural-language-driven PPA optimization and hardware design flows without model retraining.

## pqac-00000033

From text 'chang2023chipgpthowfar pages 3-4'.

Kaiyan Chang, Ying Wang, Haimeng Ren, Mengdi Wang, Shengwen Liang, Yinhe Han, Huawei Li, and Xiaowei Li. Chipgpt: how far are we from natural language hardware design. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14019. This article has 216 citations.

The excerpt describes ChipGPT’s pipeline for converting chip specifications into Verilog using LLMs and highlights prompt-generation and post-processing strategies relevant to demo-augmented pipelines and in-context learning for HLS. ChipGPT treats HDL = ChipGPT(specification) and uses a Specification Split and template-based Prompt Manager (pre-processing) plus an Output Manager and Enumerative Search (post-processing) to control outputs. It leverages in-context learning (ICL) — eliciting responses from a pre-trained LLM without retraining — and acknowledges LLM limitations: they are often unaware of PPA (power, performance, area) and may produce non-ideal programs. To address this, ChipGPT proposes an output manager to push generated designs toward better PPA, a bottom-top method to compose nested modules for scalability, and three control approaches: customize configuration/context prompts, modify the code-query prompt, or directly edit generated programs. Instead of direct program amendment, the system gives GPT multiple trials to produce improved code. These points are directly relevant to designing demo-augmented prompt pipelines, using ICL for HLS, and managing automated AI accelerator generation quality.

Specifically: hallucination of non-existent parts/pins, incorrect timing semantics, dataset scarcity for Verilog/VHDL training, verification gaps, synthesizability failures, blocking vs non-blocking confusion, FSM errors, and challenges for analog circuit generation including component value hallucination?"}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000034

From text 'pan2025asurveyof pages 10-12'.

Jingyu Pan, Guanglei Zhou, Chen-Chia Chang, Isaac Jacobson, Jiang Hu, and Yiran Chen. A survey of research in large language models for electronic design automation. Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3715324, doi:10.1145/3715324. This article has 109 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several EDA-specific limitations and mitigation efforts for LLMs. Pre-training corpus composition strongly affects Verilog generation quality: models pre-trained on small Verilog corpora perform substantially better even after supervised fine-tuning, indicating dataset/domain scarcity is a real issue. Agentic frameworks (e.g., RTLFixer, VerilogCoder) that combine Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), ReAct prompting, compile-error databases, and AST-based waveform tracing can significantly improve functional correctness and debugging, raising pass rates markedly versus plain LLM usage. However, RAG systems and LLMs still struggle with EDA-specific terminologies, complex workflows, and domain adaptation for backend tasks. Timing estimation is called out as especially challenging because models must capture relationships between layout, parasitics, and the mathematical precision of static timing analysis—pointing to risks around incorrect timing semantics. The survey notes datasets (EDA Corpus, ORD-QA) and many script-generation and documentation-Q&A applications, but the excerpt does not provide details on other specific failure modes (e.g., hallucinated pins/parts, synthesizability failures, blocking vs. non-blocking confusion, FSM errors, or analog component-value hallucinations), though it does acknowledge analog circuit design is intricate and begins discussion of analog applications.

Meisam Abdollahi, Seyedeh Faegheh Yeganli, Mohammad (Amir) Baharloo, and Amirali Baniasadi. Hardware design and verification with large language models: a scoping review, challenges, and open issues. Electronics, 14:120, Dec 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/electronics14010120, doi:10.3390/electronics14010120. This article has 103 citations.

The excerpt identifies several principal limitations of current LLMs for hardware design and verification. LLMs lack deep, domain-specific understanding of HDLs (Verilog, VHDL, SystemVerilog, UVM) and require fine-tuning on large HDL/verification datasets. Their formal reasoning is weak: they struggle with formal verification, model checking, temporal logic, SVA, and finite state machines, which undermines correct timing semantics and FSM correctness. Code-generation weaknesses include producing non–synthesis-ready HDL and failing to optimize for real-world constraints (timing, power, area). Error-detection and debugging are limited: LLMs do not reliably catch subtle HDL bugs such as incorrect state-machine transitions, misaligned clock-domain crossings, or resource contention, and need training on common verification failures. Verification automation and coverage analysis are immature: LLMs should better recognize coverage gaps and generate tests, and they must integrate with simulation/synthesis/formal tools and waveform data to act on concrete results. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss hallucination of non-existent discrete parts/pins, blocking vs. non-blocking assignment confusion by name, or analog component-value hallucination, so those specific issues are not directly addressed here.

It also emphasizes the black-box/interpretability problem that reduces engineer trust and the value of explainability techniques (attention visualization, XAI, intermediate representations) and expert feedback loops. Integration challenges are noted: LLMs must interoperate with EDA tools, file formats, data structures, and APIs, necessitating robust interfaces. The excerpt supports concerns about dataset scarcity for Verilog/VHDL, the need for verification, and interpretability, but it does not explicitly discuss many of the specific failure modes you listed (e.g., hallucinated parts/pins, incorrect timing semantics, blocking vs non-blocking confusion, FSM synthesis errors, synthesizability failures, or analog component value hallucination). Those specific issues are not present in the provided text and would require additional sources or empirical examples.

Hardware design and security needs attention: from survey to path forward. ArXiv, Apr 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.08854, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2504.08854. This article has 4 citations.

The excerpt documents several practical limitations of current LLMs when applied to hardware design. LLMs can handle simple repairs and generate helpful, user-friendly explanations (about 71% correctness for compile-time error explanations), but they face syntax and higher-level semantic challenges in HDL code and require design patterns (role specification, hierarchical design) and error-feedback loops to perform well. LLMs can outperform existing tools on simple security-relevant bug fixes, but they "struggled with more complex issues like race conditions and multi-line fixes." Success rates improve substantially with detailed prompt engineering, RAG/ReAct-style retrieval, and integration with evaluation frameworks (AutoEval) and EDA tools. For analog design, LLM-based agents achieve good results when backed by iterative checks (requirement, simulation/operating-point, DC sweep, function checks) and by archiving modular subcircuits, implying that verification and simulation remain essential to catch issues. The paper explicitly recommends hybrid methods, hardware-specific fine-tuning, and better prompt strategies. The excerpt does not explicitly confirm some of the user’s specific failure modes (e.g., hallucination of non-existent pins/parts, blocking vs. non-blocking confusion, FSM-specific errors, explicit dataset scarcity statistics for Verilog/VHDL, or concrete synthesizability-failure rates), though the noted "high-level semantic challenges," race conditions, and need for verification/EDA integration imply gaps in correctness, timing semantics, and verification breadth that must be addressed.

Electronics, 14:120, Dec 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/electronics14010120, doi:10.3390/electronics14010120. This article has 103 citations.

The excerpt highlights several limitations and open issues for applying LLMs to hardware design. A primary barrier is data: high-quality, annotated hardware design and verification datasets are scarce, often proprietary, and heterogeneous (HDL, schematics, docs), making cohesive training sets difficult. Training is resource-intensive and domain-specific knowledge is required, so models struggle without domain adaptation. Verification and correctness remain challenging—while approaches like VeriRectify (using simulator diagnostics to iteratively fix generated Verilog) and PPA checks have improved outcomes for certain designs, integration of LLMs into formal verification is still evolving and often manual. Frameworks like Ciri show promise for configuration validation but have limits. The review calls for more sophisticated models, continued benchmarking (e.g., Verilog code generation), and tighter integration with existing toolchains to address verification gaps, synthesizability and other reliability issues. Overall, the text supports concerns about dataset scarcity, verification gaps, and the need for domain adaptation and improved verification workflows when using LLMs for hardware design.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000040

From text 'sheth2506protocolllmrtlbenchmark pages 5-6'.

Arnav Sheth, Ivaxi Sheth, and Mario Fritz. Protocolllm: rtl benchmark for systemverilog generation of communication protocols. ArXiv, Jun 2506. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.07945, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2506.07945. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt documents several concrete failure modes of LLMs when generating RTL/SystemVerilog for communication protocols. Models frequently produce incomplete state transitions and timing violations that are "not detectable through syntax or synthesis checks alone," leading to "subtle protocol-level misbehaviors and logic flaws." Performance is protocol-dependent: simpler protocols (SPI, AXI4-Lite) are generated more reliably, while temporally complex protocols (UART, I2C) expose deeper weaknesses. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) increased functional intent in some cases but "had a higher tendency to fail waveform simulation" and can cause "semantic degradation, where models produce code that is less consistent or structurally flawed." Synthesis-level anomalies — unusually large area or abnormal power profiles — often indicate underlying logic or structural problems in generated RTL. There are also clear domain gaps: many code-finetuned models lack HDL in their training data, producing "out-of-distribution behavior" (e.g., DeepCoder-14B produced Python instead of SystemVerilog). The excerpt does not explicitly mention misuse of blocking vs non-blocking assignments or hallucination of module ports/signals, indicating those failure modes are not directly documented here and represent gaps to check in further evaluation.


---

## pqac-00000041

From text 'deng2025scalertlscalingllms pages 8-9'.

Chenhui Deng, Yun-Da Tsai, Guan-Ting Liu, Zhongzhi Yu, and Haoxing Ren. Scalertl: scaling llms with reasoning data and test-time compute for accurate rtl code generation. ArXiv, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.05566, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2506.05566. This article has 18 citations.

The excerpt emphasizes common pitfalls LLMs face when generating RTL/Verilog: strict syntax and functional correctness requirements, self-contained module definitions, and the need for valid Verilog code. The paper explicitly asks analyzers to identify ‘‘Complex syntax requirements,’’ ‘‘Subtle logic implementation details,’’ ‘‘Problem interpretation challenges,’’ ‘‘Timing-sensitive operations,’’ ‘‘Signal width considerations,’’ and ‘‘Tricky Verilog construct usage.’’ These categories capture many concrete failure modes—errors in state-machine sequencing, mis-implementation of handshake/timing protocols, incorrect use of blocking versus non-blocking assignments, signal-width mismatches, and misuse of uncommon Verilog constructs. The authors also note that domain knowledge and explicit RTL rules provided by hardware engineers can improve model reasoning. Finally, the work benchmarks models (showing differences across approaches and improvements from ScaleRTL and test-time scaling), implying that model/data/benchmarking choices affect performance. This excerpt therefore provides a checklist of problem areas to investigate (syntax, semantics, timing, widths, construct edge-cases, interpretation) and suggests that engineering rules and model scaling are practical mitigations.


---

## pqac-00000042

From text 'ravindran2025surveyandbenchmarking pages 13-15'.

Arun Ravindran, Aditya Patra, Vahid Babaey, and Suresh Purini. Survey and benchmarking of large language models for rtl code generation: techniques and open challenges. Unknown journal, Sep 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202509.1681.v1, doi:10.20944/preprints202509.1681.v1.

The excerpt analyzes recurring LLM failure modes in RTL generation, illustrated by the persistently failing radix2_div and FSM failures (Prob154_fsm_ps2data). Key issues include specification ambiguity (models choosing incompatible but valid variants), cycle-accuracy and FSM mis-sequencing (off-by-one or misordered per-cycle steps and incorrect sequencing of start/active/done), and bit-width arithmetic mistakes (e.g., implementing 8-bit compares when a 9-bit remainder path is required). Finalization steps are often omitted (last quotient/remainder commit), and signed-division sign rules are frequently mishandled. Handshake drift appears as misuse of res_ready/res_valid and confusion between level vs. edge signalling, causing deadlocks or stale outputs. The report also highlights syntax-versus-semantics errors such as using blocking assignments in sequential logic and simplifying borrow-detection to a plain compare. Practical prompt/testbench fixes made during RTLLM included adding missing signals (res_ready), fixing module-name mismatches, clarifying shift vs. rotate semantics, and correcting instantiation order—indicating hallucinated or mismatched ports/signals and prompt misalignment are real issues. The excerpt does not discuss dataset size or Verilog training data scarcity explicitly.


---

## pqac-00000043

From text 'ravindran2025surveyandbenchmarking pages 16-18'.

Arun Ravindran, Aditya Patra, Vahid Babaey, and Suresh Purini. Survey and benchmarking of large language models for rtl code generation: techniques and open challenges. Unknown journal, Sep 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202509.1681.v1, doi:10.20944/preprints202509.1681.v1.

The excerpt identifies multiple failure modes and root causes relevant to LLM-generated RTL. Explicitly noted are subtle corner cases such as off-by-one errors, algebraic incompleteness, and state-space underspecification that lead to FSM mis-sequencing and oversimplified state representations. Protocol-related failures appear as timing drift, invalid outputs, and missing recovery sequences, indicating handshake/protocol drift risks when protocol semantics are ambiguous. The text highlights ambiguity in free-form natural-language specs and misinterpretation during generation, which can manifest as hallucinated or incorrect ports/signals and incorrect integration (memory maps, IRQs). While not naming blocking vs non-blocking assignment misuse, the authors call out bit-level manipulations, concurrency, and algebraic incompleteness as domains where LLMs lack robustness—these are plausible sources of assignment-semantic mistakes. Dataset and benchmark gaps are emphasized: "datasets that rarely link spec→RTL→verification→PPA," implying limited training/validation data for end-to-end Verilog tasks. Recommended mitigations include schema-driven, executable specs, automated protocol/invariant checkers, counterexample-guided repair loops, symbolic/AST aids, persistent error memory, and pairing LLMs with simulation/formal feedback to iteratively fix errors.


---

## pqac-00000044

From text 'sandal2401zeroshotrtlcode pages 3-4'.

Selim Sandal and Ismail Akturk. Zero-shot rtl code generation with attention sink augmented large language models. ArXiv, Jan 2401. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2401.08683, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2401.08683. This article has 9 citations.

The excerpt identifies several concrete failure modes for LLMs applied to RTL (Verilog) code generation. It documents hallucinations and nonsensical or incomplete code outputs, explicit “Variable type and size mismatch” errors caused by context-sensitive tokenization (e.g., "a model might incorrectly tokenize ’11’ as ’111’"). It highlights corrupt output produced when long specifications exceed the model’s attention window — "crucial tokens from the initial sections might fall outside the window’s reach" — leading to loss of context. The text also notes an "Inability to capture intended hardware behavior," with examples such as models "overlooking the RTL design principle of avoiding the direct assignment of a single element from one buffer to multiple positions in another buffer" and "the generation of unnecessary ports." Finally, the excerpt points to data scarcity as a bottleneck: there is a "limited availability of well-annotated RTL code" suitable for instruction-tuning and fine-tuning. The excerpt does not explicitly mention FSM mis-sequencing, handshake-protocol drift, blocking vs non-blocking assignment misuse, or state-space oversimplification by name, though related context-loss and behavior-adherence issues could lead to such faults.

## pqac-00000045

From text 'ravindran2025surveyandbenchmarking pages 15-16'.

Arun Ravindran, Aditya Patra, Vahid Babaey, and Suresh Purini. Survey and benchmarking of large language models for rtl code generation: techniques and open challenges. Unknown journal, Sep 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202509.1681.v1, doi:10.20944/preprints202509.1681.v1.

The excerpt documents concrete LLM failure modes in RTL generation. It highlights FSM mis-sequencing ("State control: Incorrect sequencing of start/active/done leads to premature termination or extra cycles.") and state-space oversimplification ("State-space oversimplification: Collapsing required states skips timing gaps and induces premature transitions"), which cause premature transitions and misaligned temporal behavior. Handshake-related errors appear frequently: misuse of ready/valid and level-vs-edge confusion can produce deadlocks or stale outputs ("Handshake logic: Misuse of res_ready/res_valid and level vs. edge confusion produce deadlocks or stale outputs."), and handshake timing drift desynchronizes modules ("Handshake timing drift: Valid/ready asserted one cycle early/late desynchronizes modules..."). Blocking vs. non-blocking assignment misuse is explicitly noted as causing races and sim/synth mismatches ("Blocking vs. non-blocking misuse: Using = in sequential logic (or mixing with <=) induces races and sim/synth mismatches."). The report also documents Moore/Mealy confusion that produces combinational glitches. The excerpt does not discuss hallucination of module ports/signals or data scarcity in Verilog training datasets, so those failure modes are not evidenced here.

## pqac-00000046

From text 'thakur2024verigenalarge pages 19-22'.

Shailja Thakur, Baleegh Ahmad, Hammond Pearce, Benjamin Tan, Brendan Dolan-Gavitt, Ramesh Karri, and Siddharth Garg. Verigen: a large language model for verilog code generation. Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3643681, doi:10.1145/3643681. This article has 598 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt documents several concrete failure modes observed when LLMs generate RTL/Verilog. Models sometimes introduce incorrect constructs (e.g., "erroneously introduced an initial block, which is not meant for sequential logic, resulting in non-synthesizable code."), and miss required control logic (e.g., "it did not implement the wrap-around from 12 back to 1"). Bit-manipulation errors appear ("did not concatenate the most significant bits with the feedback value.") and indexing/coverage errors occur in shift operations ("either do not cover all values of the shift or assign incorrect bit positions."). LLMs can produce incorrect expressions between inputs ("fail to form correct expressions between input bits."). Ambiguities in problem specs (synchronous vs asynchronous reset) let models choose different, potentially incorrect variants ("the LLMs are free to produce any variation." and "we verify whether an active-high reset results in the correct value at the output, but we do not test the asynchronous/synchronous and other corner cases."). The authors attribute some failures to dataset limitations ("lack of diversity in the training corpus.") and note overall functional incompleteness ("None of the LLMs ... were able to generate code that achieved full functional correctness."). They also report that including textbook content improves understanding ("Textbooks provide an array of examples..."). Handshake-protocol drift and explicit blocking vs non-blocking assignment misuse are not directly described in this excerpt.

## pqac-00000047

From text 'sheth2506protocolllmrtlbenchmark pages 1-2'.

Arnav Sheth, Ivaxi Sheth, and Mario Fritz. Protocolllm: rtl benchmark for systemverilog generation of communication protocols. ArXiv, Jun 2506. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.07945, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2506.07945. This article has 2 citations.

The ProtocolLLM paper evaluates LLMs on SystemVerilog generation for communication protocols and emphasizes failures tied to timing, concurrency, and synthesizability rather than only syntax. Their pipeline rejects outputs that fail any of three stages: syntax checking, logic synthesis, or functional simulation. The authors stress that protocol RTL requires strict timing semantics, correct FSM design, signal coordination (including multi-signal handshakes like Ready/Busy/ACK and MOSI/MISO), and electrical/timing constraints (clock polarity/phase). They explicitly observe that most models fail to generate SystemVerilog implementations that meet timing constraints. The benchmark therefore assesses not only syntactic correctness but also waveform-level functional verification and PPA (performance, power, area) implications for deployability on FPGAs/ASICs. Overall, the excerpt indicates LLM failures concentrate on timing and concurrency correctness, synthesizability, and functional behavior under protocol-level interactions, rather than just surface-level code shape.

## pqac-00000048

From text 'liu2023chipnemodomainadaptedllms pages 1-2'.

Mingjie Liu, Teodor-Dumitru Ene, Robert Kirby, Chris Cheng, Nathaniel Pinckney, Rongjian Liang, Jonah Alben, Himyanshu Anand, Sanmitra Banerjee, Ismet Bayraktaroglu, Bonita Bhaskaran, Bryan Catanzaro, Arjun Chaudhuri, Sharon Clay, Bill Dally, Laura Dang, Parikshit Deshpande, Siddhanth Dhodhi, Sameer Halepete, Eric Hill, Jiashang Hu, Sumit Jain, Ankit Jindal, Brucek Khailany, George Kokai, Kishor Kunal, Xiaowei Li, Charley Lind, Hao Liu, Stuart Oberman, Sujeet Omar, Ghasem Pasandi, Sreedhar Pratty, Jonathan Raiman, Ambar Sarkar, Zhengjiang Shao, Hanfei Sun, Pratik P Suthar, Varun Tej, Walker Turner, Kaizhe Xu, and Haoxing Ren. Chipnemo: domain-adapted llms for chip design. ArXiv, Oct 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2311.00176, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2311.00176. This article has 342 citations.

The excerpt describes ChipNeMo's domain-adaptive approach: techniques include domain-adaptive tokenization, continued pretraining, instruction alignment, and domain-adapted retrieval. The training/alignment data includes a 24B-token domain-relevant component alongside larger internet/code corpora. The authors trained multiple sizes and report that their largest model, ChipNeMo-70B, "outperforms the highly capable GPT-4 on two of our use cases, namely engineering assistant chatbot and EDA scripts generation," while remaining competitive on bug summarization and analysis. The evaluated applications are an engineering assistant chatbot (for GPU ASIC and architecture engineers), EDA script generation (for two domain-specific tools using Python and Tcl for VLSI timing analysis tasks specified in English), and bug summarization/analysis. The excerpt does not provide numeric accuracy or exact metric values for EDA script generation in these pages; it only states relative performance (ChipNeMo-70B > GPT-4 on the two use cases) and describes the domain-adaptation and alignment process.


---

## pqac-00000049

From text 'sharma2024assessingeconomicviability pages 2-3'.

Assessing Economic Viability: A Comparative Analysis of Total Cost of Ownership for Domain-Adapted Large Language Models versus State-of-the-art Counterparts in Chip Design Coding Assistance This article has 8 citations.

The paper reports ChipNeMo as a domain-adapted LLM (70B parameters) built on LLaMA2. For Domain Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT) the authors used 24 billion tokens overall, although only 120 million tokens were relevant to the EDA tool tasks; processing those 120M tokens took 100 GPU hours. Subsequent supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a curated EDA subset required about 4 GPU hours to process ~5 million tokens. In benchmarked EDA script generation tasks, ChipNeMo outperformed general-purpose models: it achieved 79% accuracy, avoided syntax and logical errors, and had the fastest inference among evaluated models. ChatGPT-4 Turbo (referred to as ChatGPT-4 Turbo) reached about 70% accuracy with a low hallucination rate (~2%), while Anthropic’s Claude 3 had ~68% accuracy and a higher hallucination rate (~11%). The results indicate that domain-adaptive pretraining (even if only a modestly sized task-specific token subset was used) yielded measurable gains in EDA script generation accuracy and robustness compared to general-purpose models.


---

## pqac-00000050

From text 'liu2023chipnemodomainadaptedllms pages 18-20'.

Mingjie Liu, Teodor-Dumitru Ene, Robert Kirby, Chris Cheng, Nathaniel Pinckney, Rongjian Liang, Jonah Alben, Himyanshu Anand, Sanmitra Banerjee, Ismet Bayraktaroglu, Bonita Bhaskaran, Bryan Catanzaro, Arjun Chaudhuri, Sharon Clay, Bill Dally, Laura Dang, Parikshit Deshpande, Siddhanth Dhodhi, Sameer Halepete, Eric Hill, Jiashang Hu, Sumit Jain, Ankit Jindal, Brucek Khailany, George Kokai, Kishor Kunal, Xiaowei Li, Charley Lind, Hao Liu, Stuart Oberman, Sujeet Omar, Ghasem Pasandi, Sreedhar Pratty, Jonathan Raiman, Ambar Sarkar, Zhengjiang Shao, Hanfei Sun, Pratik P Suthar, Varun Tej, Walker Turner, Kaizhe Xu, and Haoxing Ren. The excerpt reports that domain-adaptive pretraining (DAPT) provides substantial gains: "significant enhancement achieved solely through DAPT training" and DAPT plus model alignment improves domain-specific performance (notably for EDA script generation). SteerLM alignment is helpful on hard benchmarks ("SteerLM training helps on the “hard” benchmark"), while simply adding domain-specific instructional data to SFT gave only marginal benefit and sometimes degraded SteerLM-trained model behavior ("adding domain-specific instructional data to our SFT training led to only marginal improvement on the engineering assistant task." "insufficient adaptation of our data to the SteerLM labeling system"). Retrieval augmentation (RAG) improved accuracy for easy and medium EDA script tasks ("This helped in improving the accuracy of “easy” and “medium” difficulty benchmark") but reduced accuracy on hard tasks ("On the “hard” benchmark, a degradation in the accuracy is noticed as compared to non-RAG model."). Reported evaluation numbers (engineering assistant/chat metrics in the excerpt) show ChipNeMo variants outperforming GPT-4 without retrieval (GPT-4 No = 2.84; ChipNeMo-70B-SteerG No = 5.12; ChipNeMo-70B-Steer No = 4.80). An ablation with GPT-4 using the full 32k context indicates hierarchical summarization had minimal impact. The excerpt does not mention the "24 billion tokens" pretraining quantity, so that specific token-count result is not available here.

The excerpt reports that domain-adaptive pretraining (DAPT) substantially improves in-domain design knowledge and retrieval quality. The authors built a RAG-based engineering assistant benchmark of 88 questions using a datastore of ~1.8K documents (≈67K passages). Their domain-adapted retriever performed roughly 2x better than e5 small unsupervised and ~30% better than Sentence Transformer. Human evaluation (7-point Likert) shows ChipNeMo-70B-Steer outperforms GPT-4 across all categories (including RAG hits and misses) and exceeds the similarly sized LLaMA2-70B-Chat in both model-only and RAG settings (gains of 3.31 and 1.81). RAG consistently boosts scores for all models (numeric gains reported). For EDA script generation, they evaluated Python- and Tcl-based tool benchmarks (sizes listed in Table 2) and found ChipNeMo-70B-Steer significantly outperforms off-the-shelf GPT-4 and LLaMA2-70B-Chat, especially on a proprietary Python tool where general LLMs struggled without API knowledge. The excerpt does not mention a 24B-token DAPT corpus or exact token counts.

## pqac-00000053

From text 'liu2023chipnemodomainadaptedllms pages 4-5'.

Mingjie Liu, Teodor-Dumitru Ene, Robert Kirby, Chris Cheng, Nathaniel Pinckney, Rongjian Liang, Jonah Alben, Himyanshu Anand, Sanmitra Banerjee, Ismet Bayraktaroglu, Bonita Bhaskaran, Bryan Catanzaro, Arjun Chaudhuri, Sharon Clay, Bill Dally, Laura Dang, Parikshit Deshpande, Siddhanth Dhodhi, Sameer Halepete, Eric Hill, Jiashang Hu, Sumit Jain, Ankit Jindal, Brucek Khailany, George Kokai, Kishor Kunal, Xiaowei Li, Charley Lind, Hao Liu, Stuart Oberman, Sujeet Omar, Ghasem Pasandi, Sreedhar Pratty, Jonathan Raiman, Ambar Sarkar, Zhengjiang Shao, Hanfei Sun, Pratik P Suthar, Varun Tej, Walker Turner, Kaizhe Xu, and Haoxing Ren. Chipnemo: domain-adapted llms for chip design. ArXiv, Oct 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2311.00176, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2311.00176. This article has 342 citations.

The excerpt reports ChipNeMo domain-adaptive pretraining (DAPT) findings but does not provide token-counts like "24 billion tokens" nor explicit EDA script generation accuracy numbers or direct numeric comparisons to GPT-4. Key points: they adapted the LLaMA2 tokenizer adding ~9K tokens, improving tokenization efficiency by ~1.6–3.3% on chip datasets. To mitigate hallucinations in an "engineering assistant chatbot" context they propose retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). DAPT substantially improves in-domain task performance, with larger base models yielding larger in-domain gains; they evaluated 7B, 13B, and 70B models and ran application evaluations using ChipNeMo-70B (aligned via SteerLM). However, applying DAPT directly to a chat-aligned model (LLaMA2-Chat) degraded alignment and made the model unusable for downstream tasks. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA/PEFT) underperformed full-parameter DAPT despite experiments with small and large adapters. Training used 128 A100 GPUs and DAPT accounted for under ~1.5% of overall costs. The excerpt therefore offers process, scale, and qualitative performance insights but lacks the specific quantitative comparisons and EDA generation accuracy asked in the question.

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive summary table of ~15 key publications on LLM-based hardware design and code generation (2022-2025). Columns should include: Reference (short citation), Year, Category (e.g., HDL Generation, Benchmark, Agentic/Self-repair, Analog, Domain Adaptation, Accelerator Design), Key Contribution (1-2 sentences), Benchmark/Evaluation, and Key Metric/Result. Include: (1) ChipGPT - Chang et al. 2023, four-stage zero-code framework, PPA optimization, 47% area reduction; (2) ChipNeMo - Liu et al. 2023, domain-adapted LLM 70B, 24.1B tokens DAPT, outperforms GPT-4 on EDA scripts; (3) VerilogEval - Liu et al. 2023, 156-problem benchmark from HDLBits, pass@k evaluation; (4) RTLLM - Lu et al. 2024, open RTL benchmark with 30 designs, syntax/functionality/quality goals; (5) AutoChip/extended - Blocklove et al. 2025, feedback-driven Verilog generation with EDA tool feedback, 5.8% improvement over zero-shot; (6) MAGE - Zhao et al. 2025, multi-agent RTL engine, 95.7% pass@1 on VerilogEval-V2; (7) RTLFixer - Tsai et al. 2024, RAG+ReAct syntax repair, 98.5% fix rate, 32.3% pass@1 improvement; (8) VeriGen - Thakur et al. 2024, fine-tuned CodeGen-16B on Verilog, open-source; (9) AnalogCoder - Lai et al. 2025, training-free LLM agent for analog circuits via Python/PySpice, 20/24 tasks solved; (10) LaMAGIC - Chang et al. 2024, LM-based analog topology generation, 96% success rate; (11) GPT4AIGChip - Fu et al. 2023, demo-augmented prompt pipeline for AI accelerators, in-context learning for HLS; (12) Chip-Chat - Blocklove et al. 2023, first AI-written HDL tapeout, 8-bit processor; (13) RTLCoder - Liu et al. 2025, open-source 7B model outperforming GPT-3.5; (14) Masala-CHAI - Bhandari et al. 2024, 7500 schematic analog SPICE netlist dataset; (15) Revisiting VerilogEval/VerilogEval v2 - Pinckney et al. 2025, GPT-4o achieves 63% pass rate."}
